EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced quarter size hail in Dixon County in extreme northeast Nebraska on the evening of September 19th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management and spotters reported flash flooding across Saline County. The area received between 4 to 7 inches of rainfall and this led to the flash flooding. The flooding caused several streets and highways to be closed, including Nebraska Highway 15 near the Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over southwest Nebraska and moved east. A peak wind gust of 63 mph was measured at the McCook ASOS.

A low pressure system intensified as it tracked east across the central Rockies. The winter storm produced moderate to heavy snow and gusty north to northeast winds of 25 to 35 mph. Considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities created significant impacts to holiday travel across much of the western Nebraska Panhandle.  Snow accumulations ranged from four to 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow was reported, heaviest south of Kimball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough over northern Nebraska produced a brief period of moderate to heavy snow.  Gusty north winds of 35 to 45 mph produced considerable blowing snow and very low visibilities across portions of the northern Nebraska Panhandle.  Snow accumulations from six to twelve inches, heaviest along the Pine Ridge.  One to three foot snow drifts, closed several county and state roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from six to 12 inches, heaviest along the Pine Ridge and along a line from six miles west of Chadron to Chadron.  Snow drifts up to three feet were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought rain, followed by freezing rain, sleet and snow to portions of western and north central Nebraska. The storm began as rain early in the afternoon on December 14th, and transitioned to freezing rain, sleet, then snow, during the overnight hours and daytime hours on December 15th.  The heaviest snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 9 inches across north central Nebraska. Freezing rain and sleet caused additional impacts across eastern portions of north central Nebraska, causing very icy roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across western Cherry County ranged from 4 to 6 inches. North winds of 20 to 30 mph increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph caused blowing and drifting with low visibility during the overnight hours into the morning hours on December 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved from the northern Plains on the morning of the 16th to the mid Mississippi River Valley by the evening. Behind this system, across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa strong northwest developed during the morning and peaked in intensity during the early afternoon. Many locations across northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa recorded wind gusts greater than 60 mph with sustained winds greater than 40 mph during this time. The strong winds created some minor damage, power outages and blowing dust. Winds diminished during the evening hours on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow with blowing and drifting to portions of Sheridan County on December 22-23, 2014.  The highest snowfall totals were across northwest Sheridan County ranging from 12 inches near Hayes Springs and 6 inches near Rushville.  Across the remainder of the county, amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches. Northwest winds increasing to 20 to 30 mph on December 23rd, caused blowing and drifting snow and visibilities as low as a quarter mile at times during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement in Rushville reported 6 inches of snowfall.  Visibility was low, ranging from a quarter to half mile along with blowing and drifting during the morning hours on December 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the month of June greatly improved the long-standing drought situation across southwest Nebraska. Precipitation at cooperative observer sites ranged from 1.9 to 6.6 inches above normal for the month. As a result, Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought reported at the beginning of the month improved to Moderate (D1) Drought by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hitchcock County improved from Severe (D2) to Moderate (D1) Drought during the month of June as a result of heavy rainfall during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong shortwave trough moved into the northern Plains during the day on Monday July 7th. This allowed for a cold front to advance southeast across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening hours. Due to persistent clouds, as well as frontal passage the previous day resulting in limited moisture, the atmosphere was only moderately unstable for July standards. A band of elevated showers and thunderstorms slowly intensified as they moved southeast across the area. The thunderstorms become the most intense over far southwest Iowa and southeast Nebraska closer to a stalled frontal boundary. The thunderstorms quickly moved into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri by late evening ending the severe weather threat.

The thunderstorms continued southeast through the night exiting into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed after midnight into the early morning on August 20th. The thunderstorms developed behind a weak cold front that was moving into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri. The thunderstorms were non-severe, but isolated reports of hail were received.

Isolated thunderstorms develop just behind a weakening cold front as the low level jet increased during the evening of August 24th. The thunderstorms were generally non-severe and continued into the morning of August 25th, but an isolated microburst did occur in parts of Jefferson County in southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manger reported that a 2 year old 60 by 90 foot barn was destroyed at a rural residence.

A seasonably strong upper level storm system swept across the northern Plains during the day on Sunday August 31st. Ahead of the storm system low pressure developed over the high Plains and allowed for warm and moist air to pool over the mid Missouri River Valley creating strong instability by mid to late afternoon. As a cold front and associated upper level jet streak interacted with the instability, thunderstorms developed from northeast Nebraska into Northern Kansas. These thunderstorms moved east through the rest of the afternoon and evening hours, crossing eastern Nebraska and western Iowa produced mainly hail and wind damage, but heavy rainfall also occurred in parts of the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal precipitation occurred across most of the region during the month of August. A particularly wet period occurred at the end of the month though as two days of repeated heavy rainfall led to river flooding across parts of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. Most of the flooding was minor, but did cause some road closures and crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated thunderstorms on the 27th and 28th of August caused heavy rainfall and the West Fork of the Big Blue river to go into flood. The river rose above flood stage on the morning of the 29th, the river created and fell below flood stage in early early September. The flooding was generally minor and confined to low-lying areas along the river.

A fast moving but potent storm system moved south from the eastern Dakota into Iowa during the day on the 18th. A cold front moved rapidly south across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa in the early morning. Behind the cold front, winds increased from the northwest across the area during the morning as high pressure built into the area. Northwest sustained winds of 20 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph were common, but isolated locations recorded higher winds greater than 58 mph with some light damage reported. The winds decreased by late afternoon as the storm system pushed off to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured in Duncan, but several high wind reports were received across Platte County including a 60 mph wind gusts 2 miles northwest of Columbus and a 58 mph wind gust was measured by the AWOS at the Columbus airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system dropped southeast from Manitoba, Canada into the Great Lakes on this Thursday. As the low moved into the Great Lakes, its forward progress slowed considerably. The upper-level flow was highly amplified and it was approaching the axis of the upper-level trough. The slowing resulted in a very tight pressure gradient. At noon, the central pressure of the low was 993 mb over Lake Superior, with the pressure of the high 1040 mb over Idaho. The result was an extremely windy day, with the strongest winds occurring from late morning to late afternoon. Peak sustained winds were above 40 mph for at least one hour across the region. At Hastings, Grand Island, and Ord, winds were sustained at more than 40 mph for up to 4 hours. The highest peak wind gusts were mostly between 55 and 65 mph. The highest peak gusts included 65 mph at Ohiowa, in Fillmore county, and 73 mph at Norman, in Kearney county. Four small grass fires occurred across South Central Nebraska as well. Blowing dust was widespread, with the visibility as low as 2 miles in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations from surrounding counties measured sustained speeds of 40 MPH or higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the fifth wind event in two weeks, finishing a very windy period. This wind event was due to another Alberta Clipper, diving from North Dakota into the Great Lakes. The associated cold front moved through early in the afternoon and the pressure gradient was tight between the 990 mb low and the 1040 mb high over Alberta and Saskatchewan. As with the majority of events earlier this month, peak sustained winds were mostly between 30 and 39 mph, with maximum gusts generally 50-56 mph. Sustained winds at Aurora and Grand Island briefly hit 40 mph. A couple locations managed to gust to at least 58 mph. The highest peak gust was 61 mph near Davenport, in Thayer county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust was measured at a mesonet site 2 miles north-northeast of Davenport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although minor accumulation persisted well into the day across parts of South Central Nebraska on Monday the 15th, one of the first ���impactful��� snow events of the 2014-15 winter season occurred mainly during the pre-dawn hours as generally 1-4 inches of fairly wet snow targeted several counties primarily west of the Highway 281 corridor. Within this area, locations roughly 15 miles either side of a line from Ord-Elm Creek-Oxford received the most concentrated swath of steady, wind-blown snow, the majority of which fell between 2-8 a.m. Even more specifically, some of the overall-worst early-morning travel conditions focused within a narrow, north-south oriented snow band over Phelps and western Buffalo counties. Including the generally light accumulation that lingered well into the day, a few of the top storm total snowfall totals from NWS cooperative observers featured 4.0��� in Ord, 2.5��� in Arcadia, 2.5��� six miles north-northwest of Oxford and 1.5��� at Holdrege. In addition, a social media report yielded 3.0��� four miles south of Odessa. While actual amounts were not overly-significant in most places, the briefly-intense snowfall rates caused some issues, especially given that sustained north-northwest wind averaged 20-25 MPH while gusting up to around 40 MPH. ||Prior to colder air building down from the north and changing precipitation over to snow on the 15th, most of the afternoon and evening of Sunday the 14th featured scattered showers and even a few rare December thunderstorms within South Central Nebraska. Some places also experienced a fairly brief period of freezing rain and/or sleet during the overnight transition from rain to snow. In the ���big picture��� of the mid and upper levels, this event was driven by a powerful, nearly vertically-stacked, closed low pressure system that tracked from southwest-to-northeast across Kansas over the course of the night. Given that South Central Nebraska remained north and eventually northwest of the surface low this entire time, increasingly-strong north-northwest wind ushered increasingly-colder air into the area. As often occurs to the northwest of a well-organized low pressure system, the primary north-south oriented snow band focused within an area of enhanced low to mid level frontogenesis/deformation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated one to three inches of snowfall occurred across Harlan County, with greater amounts in eastern Harlan County, within a heavier snow band. This snow was combined with gusts from the north northwest up to around 40 mph, which reduced visibility. Some light snow and flurries occurred into the afternoon, but the bulk of accumulating snow was done by 9 am CST. A number of schools started late.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system dropped southeast from Manitoba, Canada into the Great Lakes on this Thursday. As the low moved into the Great Lakes, its forward progress slowed considerably. The upper-level flow was highly amplified and it was approaching the axis of the upper-level trough. The slowing resulted in a very tight pressure gradient. At noon, the central pressure of the low was 993 mb over Lake Superior, with the pressure of the high 1040 mb over Idaho. The result was an extremely windy day, with the strongest winds occurring from late morning to late afternoon. Peak sustained winds were above 40 mph for at least one hour across the region. At Hastings, Grand Island, and Ord, winds were sustained at more than 40 mph for up to 4 hours. The highest peak wind gusts were mostly between 55 and 65 mph. The highest peak gusts included 65 mph at Ohiowa, in Fillmore county, and 73 mph at Norman, in Kearney county. Four small grass fires occurred across South Central Nebraska as well. Blowing dust was widespread, with the visibility as low as 2 miles in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The York Airport AWOS measured a wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure that pushed south into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa March 1st and 2nd brought record cold temperatures to the region on north winds of 10 to 20 mph.  Air temperatures fell below zero during the overnight hours of March 1st and struggled to climb above zero on March 2nd.  In fact, the highs on March 2nd were the coldest on record for most locations for any March day.  The brisk winds and cold temperatures combined to lower wind chill values down to 20 below to 29 below zero.

Primarily during the pre-dawn hours of Thursday the 18th, most of the 30-county South Central Nebraska area picked up a minor, light coating of snow up to around one-half inch. However, the far southeast fringes of the local area, especially Nuckolls and Thayer counties, featured a more concentrated zone of higher amounts generally in the 1.5 to 3��� range. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest overnight totals featured 3.0��� at Byron and also seven miles northeast of Superior, 2.6��� in Superior, 2.1��� in Bruning and 2.0��� at Hubbell. Fortunately, light wind made this an almost ���ideal��� snowfall, as sustained easterly speeds only averaged around 5 MPH. ||Timing-wise, the vast majority of accumulation occurred between 11 p.m. and 6 a.m. as the Nuckolls/Thayer area resided along the far northwest edge of a larger snowfall swath of generally 2-5��� that mainly targeted central and northeast Kansas. On the large scale of the mid and upper levels, this fairly low-impact event was driven primarily by broad isentropic lift focused along and north of a low-amplitude shortwave trough shifting across KS and toward the MO/IA border through the night, with only a fairly weak contribution from frontogenetic forcing. At the surface, the very light wind was fostered by a weak pressure gradient on the southwest fringes of a broad high centered over the Upper Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Nebraska Rainfall Assessment and Information Network (NERAIN) observer reported 3 inches of snow, 7 miles northeast of Superior. Snow amounts were generally in the 2 to 3 inch range in the southern half of Nuckolls County. The cooperative observer in Superior reported 2.6 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly between the mid-afternoon and late-evening hours of Friday the 26th, the majority of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area received at least 1-2��� of snow, with locations mainly north and northwest of a Johnson Lake-Kearney-Grand Island-Fullerton line realizing the highest totals of 2-4���. In addition, prior to the onset of snow, most counties along and especially east of the Highway 281 corridor experienced a few hours of light freezing drizzle, slickening area roadways and resulting in numerous traffic accidents (the majority of freezing drizzle occurred during the 2-7 p.m. time frame). Getting back to snowfall, some of the highest storm total amounts per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers featured 4.0��� at St. Paul, 3.5��� at Grand Island airport and Ord, 3.1��� at Greeley and 3.0��� at Ravenna, Belgrade and also four miles east of Scotia. Farther south and east, lower totals prevailed at places such as Hastings NWS office (1.5���), three miles north of York (1.5���) and Hebron (1.0���).  Fortunately, blowing and drifting snow was not overly-problematic given that sustained north wind generally averaged around 15 MPH or less with gusts largely under 25 MPH. ||Timing-wise, between 3-5 p.m. a broad area of mainly light to moderate snow initially spread north and northeast into South Central Nebraska, first affecting counties mainly along and west of the Highway 281 corridor, while counties farther east started seeing the aforementioned freezing drizzle. During the evening, the broad area of snow expanded to cover nearly all of South Central Nebraska, before gradually tapering off to flurries or ending from west-to-east mainly between 9 p.m. and 2 a.m. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by a lead shortwave trough ejecting northeast into the Central Plains out of the base of a larger-scale, positively-tilted parent trough anchored over the Desert Southwest and Four Corners regions. At the surface, temperatures across South Central Nebraska averaged in the 20s, as the local area resided well to the north of a cold front stretched along an axis from west TX to eastern IA at sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total snowfall of 3.5 inches occurred at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system dropped southeast from Manitoba, Canada into the Great Lakes on this Thursday. As the low moved into the Great Lakes, its forward progress slowed considerably. The upper-level flow was highly amplified and it was approaching the axis of the upper-level trough. The slowing resulted in a very tight pressure gradient. At noon, the central pressure of the low was 993 mb over Lake Superior, with the pressure of the high 1040 mb over Idaho. The result was an extremely windy day, with the strongest winds occurring from late morning to late afternoon. Peak sustained winds were above 40 mph for at least one hour across the region. At Hastings, Grand Island, and Ord, winds were sustained at more than 40 mph for up to 4 hours. The highest peak wind gusts were mostly between 55 and 65 mph. The highest peak gusts included 65 mph at Ohiowa, in Fillmore county, and 73 mph at Norman, in Kearney county. Four small grass fires occurred across South Central Nebraska as well. Blowing dust was widespread, with the visibility as low as 2 miles in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations from surrounding counties measured sustained speeds of 40 MPH or higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure that pushed south into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa March 1st and 2nd brought record cold temperatures to the region on north winds of 10 to 20 mph.  Air temperatures fell below zero during the overnight hours of March 1st and struggled to climb above zero on March 2nd.  In fact, the highs on March 2nd were the coldest on record for most locations for any March day.  The brisk winds and cold temperatures combined to lower wind chill values down to 20 below to 29 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure pushed into the mid Missouri River Valley on February 5th behind a strong storm system that moved across the central Plains.  The combination of the high over the northern plains and the storm system over the central plains created a strong pressure gradient across the area bringing brisk northwest winds.  As temperatures fell into the single digits above and below zero during the early morning hours of February 5th, the brisk winds created bitterly cold wind chill values that ranged from 15 to 25 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and warm conditions, along with gusty southwest winds, help fuel several fires near Lincoln. Temperatures that reached around 70 degrees combined with dew point readings in the teens to lower 20s lowered relative humidity values to less than 20 percent for several hours from late in the morning through mid afternoon on March 15th.  Winds were southwest at 15 to 30 mph, but shifted to the northeast at 30 to 45 mph as a cold front pressed south by mid to late afternoon.  The cooler air helped raise relative humidity readings, but only marginally until early evening when temperatures cooled more significantly.  Several fires in the area quickly burned out of control due to the dry, windy weather.  One near Lincoln, of unknown origin, caused around $200,000 worth of damage and threatened many structures was contained before doing much more damage.  The other significant fire, in Seward county west of Lincoln, started when a controlled burn got out of control.  This fire burned around 1,000 acres.  In all it was estimated that at least a half dozen good-sized grassfires in southeast Nebraska this day according to a Lincoln fire investigator.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush and grass fire burned just about everything between the Salt Creek Levee Trail, a line of train tracks to the west and West A and South Streets according to the Lincoln Journal.  It was unknown what started this fire.  The fire caused about $200,000 damage to foam board, shingles, trucks and a trailer at While Castle Roofing.  It also threatened numerous structures in the area briefly before being extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across the northern Plains on the morning of February 25th. This storm system moved along a strong temperature gradient over the central and northern Plains producing a band of accumulating snowfall over northeast Nebraska. The snow started falling during the morning hours and periods of snow, moderate at times, fell through the day ending during the evening on the 24th. Little wind occurred during the snow limiting impacts to mainly travel inconvenience. Total snowfall from the event ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general snowfall of 3 to 6 inches occurred across the county with isolated higher amounts. Specific amounts included 3.8 near Verdel and 7.1 inches at Bloomfield.

Primarily during the pre-dawn hours of Thursday the 18th, most of the 30-county South Central Nebraska area picked up a minor, light coating of snow up to around one-half inch. However, the far southeast fringes of the local area, especially Nuckolls and Thayer counties, featured a more concentrated zone of higher amounts generally in the 1.5 to 3��� range. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers, a few of the highest overnight totals featured 3.0��� at Byron and also seven miles northeast of Superior, 2.6��� in Superior, 2.1��� in Bruning and 2.0��� at Hubbell. Fortunately, light wind made this an almost ���ideal��� snowfall, as sustained easterly speeds only averaged around 5 MPH. ||Timing-wise, the vast majority of accumulation occurred between 11 p.m. and 6 a.m. as the Nuckolls/Thayer area resided along the far northwest edge of a larger snowfall swath of generally 2-5��� that mainly targeted central and northeast Kansas. On the large scale of the mid and upper levels, this fairly low-impact event was driven primarily by broad isentropic lift focused along and north of a low-amplitude shortwave trough shifting across KS and toward the MO/IA border through the night, with only a fairly weak contribution from frontogenetic forcing. At the surface, the very light wind was fostered by a weak pressure gradient on the southwest fringes of a broad high centered over the Upper Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Nebraska Rainfall Assessment and Information Network (NERAIN) observer reported 3 inches of snow near Byron. Snow amounts were generally in the 2 to 3 inch range, especially in the southern half of Thayer County. The cooperative observer in Hubbell measured 2.0 inches, while Hebron measured 1.7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures that reached the 70s combined with low dew points and southwest winds gusting over 40 mph brought conditions favorable for spreading of wildfires across eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of March 9th.  Several wildfires were reported across the region, including one that burned nearly 1,000 acres northwest of Lincoln near Branched Oak Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire, possibly started by a discarded cigarette, burned around 1,000 acres near Branched Oak Lake northwest of Lincoln.  The fire was spread rapidly by southwest winds which gusted over 40 mph, and dry conditions as temperatures reached the 70s and low dew points dropped relative humidity values below 20 percent.   The fire burned about 200 to 300 acres of State Recreation land and also damaged one home in the area before it was brought under containment.   Nineteen fire fighting departments fought the fire, and one firefighter was treated for smoke inhalation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the third is a series of four systems that produced very winds conditions, roughly every other day, over the seven-day period from January 14th to the 20th. The first event on the 14th was an advisory event. The second on the 16th was a warning event. The event on this Saturday was primarily an advisory event, but a few high wind gusts of 58 mph or greater did occur as a result of an Alberta Clipper that dove from the Canadian Prairies southeastward into Minnesota, Iowa, and eventually Illinois by evening. These strong winds occurred behind the cold front, which moved through around daybreak. Sustained winds of 30-39 mph were widespread, along with gusts of 45-55 mph. Lexington, Kearney, and Aurora did measure sustained winds of at least 40 mph. However, only Aurora satisfied the 1 hour criterion. A few locations managed to record gusts of 58 mph or more. The highest wind gust was 65 mph, measured at Ohiowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds as high as 43 MPH were measured by the Aurora Airport AWOS.

Arctic high pressure that pushed south into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa March 1st and 2nd brought record cold temperatures to the region on north winds of 10 to 20 mph.  Air temperatures fell below zero during the overnight hours of March 1st and struggled to climb above zero on March 2nd.  In fact, the highs on March 2nd were the coldest on record for most locations for any March day.  The brisk winds and cold temperatures combined to lower wind chill values down to 20 below to 29 below zero.

Arctic high pressure pushed into the mid Missouri River Valley on February 5th behind a strong storm system that moved across the central Plains.  The combination of the high over the northern plains and the storm system over the central plains created a strong pressure gradient across the area bringing brisk northwest winds.  As temperatures fell into the single digits above and below zero during the early morning hours of February 5th, the brisk winds created bitterly cold wind chill values that ranged from 15 to 25 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong northwest winds, sustained above 40 mph at times with gusts around 60 mph, were persistent during the afternoon of January 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong northwest winds were occasionally over 40 mph sustained, with gusts near 60 mph. Sustained winds of 48 mph were reported 4 miles northeast of Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system dropped southeast from Manitoba, Canada into the Great Lakes on this Thursday. As the low moved into the Great Lakes, its forward progress slowed considerably. The upper-level flow was highly amplified and it was approaching the axis of the upper-level trough. The slowing resulted in a very tight pressure gradient. At noon, the central pressure of the low was 993 mb over Lake Superior, with the pressure of the high 1040 mb over Idaho. The result was an extremely windy day, with the strongest winds occurring from late morning to late afternoon. Peak sustained winds were above 40 mph for at least one hour across the region. At Hastings, Grand Island, and Ord, winds were sustained at more than 40 mph for up to 4 hours. The highest peak wind gusts were mostly between 55 and 65 mph. The highest peak gusts included 65 mph at Ohiowa, in Fillmore county, and 73 mph at Norman, in Kearney county. Four small grass fires occurred across South Central Nebraska as well. Blowing dust was widespread, with the visibility as low as 2 miles in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ord Airport ASOS measured a sustained wind speed of 48 MPH.  A wind gust of 60 MPH was also measured at 12:05 p.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the third is a series of four systems that produced very winds conditions, roughly every other day, over the seven-day period from January 14th to the 20th. The first event on the 14th was an advisory event. The second on the 16th was a warning event. The event on this Saturday was primarily an advisory event, but a few high wind gusts of 58 mph or greater did occur as a result of an Alberta Clipper that dove from the Canadian Prairies southeastward into Minnesota, Iowa, and eventually Illinois by evening. These strong winds occurred behind the cold front, which moved through around daybreak. Sustained winds of 30-39 mph were widespread, along with gusts of 45-55 mph. Lexington, Kearney, and Aurora did measure sustained winds of at least 40 mph. However, only Aurora satisfied the 1 hour criterion. A few locations managed to record gusts of 58 mph or more. The highest wind gust was 65 mph, measured at Ohiowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet site located near Wood River.  Time is an estimation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the final is a series of four systems that produced very winds conditions, roughly every other day, over the seven-day period from January 14th to the 20th. The events on the 14th, 18th, and on this date, were predominantly advisory level events. The second on the 16th was a clear warning level event. On this Monday, sustained winds peaked between 30 and 39 mph, with most peak gusts between 45 and 54 mph. However, a few high wind gusts of at least 58 mph occurred. These winds primarily occurred north of a line from Kearney to Hastings to Hebron. The highest wind gust was 64 mph, measured at Grand Island. This wind event occurred behind an exceptionally long cold front stretched from the Canadian maritime provinces, through New England and the Midwest, and its western fringe was banked up against the Rockies. This front was the leading edge of an expansive arctic air mass. A weak low was collocated with the front and progressed along it, across the Dakotas into Iowa. This low intensified the pressure gradient with a 1040 mb high pressure center that followed closely behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust was measured by a mesonet site 1 mile south of Wood River.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, January 2014 marked the 20th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued some portion of this 24-county South Central Nebraska area, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. January featured the second consecutive month of below normal precipitation area-wide. In fact, according to data from nearly 50 NWS cooperative observers, roughly 60 percent of South Central Nebraska received no more than half of normal January precipitation (based on long-term January normals generally ranging between 0.40 and 0.65). Despite the continued dry stretch, there was actually very little change in drought classification across the area during January according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, thus maintaining a west (worse) to east (better) disparity. The only change of note involved southeastern Franklin and far southwestern Webster counties, which degraded from D1 Moderate to D2 Severe Drought late in the month. As a result, these areas joined the pre-existing D2 Drought area that already covered several southwestern counties including all/most of Furnas, Gosper, and Harlan, along with smaller portions of Dawson and Phelps. Meanwhile, a fairly narrow corridor of D1 Moderate Drought continued to border up against the D2 area, and covered the majority of several counties mainly west of Highway 281 including Valley, Sherman, Kearney, Phelps and Webster. Finally, nearly all locations along/east of the Highway 281 corridor in the local area including all of 13 counties and portions of others remained in Category D0 Abnormally Dry classification at month's end (considered to be one category better than true drought). ||For most of South Central Nebraska, the majority of January precipitation fell in the form of less than 5 of fairly dry snow, thus providing very little liquid moisture equivalent. Also for most of the area, the brunt of January precipitation fell on both the first and last days of the month, with very little in between. Thanks to not only a dry December and January, but also a predominantly dry November, many places within South Central Nebraska recorded one of the Top-5 driest 86-day stretches on record between the dates of Nov. 6-Jan. 30, including Grand Island airport, Kearney, Hastings, Osceola, Hebron and Holdrege. Specifically at Grand Island airport, it was the 3rd-driest Nov. 6-Jan. 30 stretch on record totaling only 0.33, and was the driest since 1980-1981. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the leanest January precipitation totals included 0.01 at Red Cloud, 0.04 at Hubbell, 0.05 at Hebron and 0.06 at Ord airport, Greeley and three miles northeast of Shelby. On the somewhat wetter side of things were stations located two miles northeast of Upland (0.43) and at Hastings airport and Clay Center (0.37). At the other official Tri-Cities airport sites, January precipitation totals featured 0.32 at Grand Island and 0.28 at Kearney. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of January, state-averaged soil moisture supplies clearly declined during the month. Topsoil moisture degraded notably since late-December, as the short to very short category increased from 33% to 60%, and the adequate category dropped to 40%. Subsoil moisture also degraded during the month but not quite as severely, increasing from 44% to 56% short to very short, while the adequate category decreased to 44%.

Arctic high pressure that pushed south into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa March 1st and 2nd brought record cold temperatures to the region on north winds of 10 to 20 mph.  Air temperatures fell below zero during the overnight hours of March 1st and struggled to climb above zero on March 2nd.  In fact, the highs on March 2nd were the coldest on record for most locations for any March day.  The brisk winds and cold temperatures combined to lower wind chill values down to 20 below to 29 below zero.

A deep low pressure system dropped southeast from Manitoba, Canada into the Great Lakes on this Thursday. As the low moved into the Great Lakes, its forward progress slowed considerably. The upper-level flow was highly amplified and it was approaching the axis of the upper-level trough. The slowing resulted in a very tight pressure gradient. At noon, the central pressure of the low was 993 mb over Lake Superior, with the pressure of the high 1040 mb over Idaho. The result was an extremely windy day, with the strongest winds occurring from late morning to late afternoon. Peak sustained winds were above 40 mph for at least one hour across the region. At Hastings, Grand Island, and Ord, winds were sustained at more than 40 mph for up to 4 hours. The highest peak wind gusts were mostly between 55 and 65 mph. The highest peak gusts included 65 mph at Ohiowa, in Fillmore county, and 73 mph at Norman, in Kearney county. Four small grass fires occurred across South Central Nebraska as well. Blowing dust was widespread, with the visibility as low as 2 miles in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations from surrounding counties measured sustained speeds of 40 MPH or higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the third is a series of four systems that produced very winds conditions, roughly every other day, over the seven-day period from January 14th to the 20th. The first event on the 14th was an advisory event. The second on the 16th was a warning event. The event on this Saturday was primarily an advisory event, but a few high wind gusts of 58 mph or greater did occur as a result of an Alberta Clipper that dove from the Canadian Prairies southeastward into Minnesota, Iowa, and eventually Illinois by evening. These strong winds occurred behind the cold front, which moved through around daybreak. Sustained winds of 30-39 mph were widespread, along with gusts of 45-55 mph. Lexington, Kearney, and Aurora did measure sustained winds of at least 40 mph. However, only Aurora satisfied the 1 hour criterion. A few locations managed to record gusts of 58 mph or more. The highest wind gust was 65 mph, measured at Ohiowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 65 MPH was measured by a mesonet site located near Ohiowa.  Time is an estimation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although minor accumulation persisted well into the day across parts of South Central Nebraska on Monday the 15th, one of the first ���impactful��� snow events of the 2014-15 winter season occurred mainly during the pre-dawn hours as generally 1-4 inches of fairly wet snow targeted several counties primarily west of the Highway 281 corridor. Within this area, locations roughly 15 miles either side of a line from Ord-Elm Creek-Oxford received the most concentrated swath of steady, wind-blown snow, the majority of which fell between 2-8 a.m. Even more specifically, some of the overall-worst early-morning travel conditions focused within a narrow, north-south oriented snow band over Phelps and western Buffalo counties. Including the generally light accumulation that lingered well into the day, a few of the top storm total snowfall totals from NWS cooperative observers featured 4.0��� in Ord, 2.5��� in Arcadia, 2.5��� six miles north-northwest of Oxford and 1.5��� at Holdrege. In addition, a social media report yielded 3.0��� four miles south of Odessa. While actual amounts were not overly-significant in most places, the briefly-intense snowfall rates caused some issues, especially given that sustained north-northwest wind averaged 20-25 MPH while gusting up to around 40 MPH. ||Prior to colder air building down from the north and changing precipitation over to snow on the 15th, most of the afternoon and evening of Sunday the 14th featured scattered showers and even a few rare December thunderstorms within South Central Nebraska. Some places also experienced a fairly brief period of freezing rain and/or sleet during the overnight transition from rain to snow. In the ���big picture��� of the mid and upper levels, this event was driven by a powerful, nearly vertically-stacked, closed low pressure system that tracked from southwest-to-northeast across Kansas over the course of the night. Given that South Central Nebraska remained north and eventually northwest of the surface low this entire time, increasingly-strong north-northwest wind ushered increasingly-colder air into the area. As often occurs to the northwest of a well-organized low pressure system, the primary north-south oriented snow band focused within an area of enhanced low to mid level frontogenesis/deformation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation originally switched from rain to freezing rain for about an hour or so, creating an initial slick, thin coat of ice on surfaces. Then a slow transition to sleet, then snow during the overnight hours. There did not appear to be a clear transition to snow, as precipitation types wavered back and forth for a couple of hours before switching to primarily snow. A total of 3 inches of snow occurred in Ord from a cooperative observer. Nebraska Rainfall Assessment and Information Network (NERAIN) reports ranged 2 to 3 inches in southern Valley County, to 4 to 6 inches in northern Valley County. This snow was combined with gusts from the north northwest up to around 40 mph, which reduced visibility. Some light snow and flurries occurred into the afternoon, but the bulk of accumulating snow was done by 8 am CST. A number of schools started late due to the wintry mix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, January 2014 marked the 20th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued some portion of this 24-county South Central Nebraska area, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. January featured the second consecutive month of below normal precipitation area-wide. In fact, according to data from nearly 50 NWS cooperative observers, roughly 60 percent of South Central Nebraska received no more than half of normal January precipitation (based on long-term January normals generally ranging between 0.40 and 0.65). Despite the continued dry stretch, there was actually very little change in drought classification across the area during January according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, thus maintaining a west (worse) to east (better) disparity. The only change of note involved southeastern Franklin and far southwestern Webster counties, which degraded from D1 Moderate to D2 Severe Drought late in the month. As a result, these areas joined the pre-existing D2 Drought area that already covered several southwestern counties including all/most of Furnas, Gosper, and Harlan, along with smaller portions of Dawson and Phelps. Meanwhile, a fairly narrow corridor of D1 Moderate Drought continued to border up against the D2 area, and covered the majority of several counties mainly west of Highway 281 including Valley, Sherman, Kearney, Phelps and Webster. Finally, nearly all locations along/east of the Highway 281 corridor in the local area including all of 13 counties and portions of others remained in Category D0 Abnormally Dry classification at month's end (considered to be one category better than true drought). ||For most of South Central Nebraska, the majority of January precipitation fell in the form of less than 5 of fairly dry snow, thus providing very little liquid moisture equivalent. Also for most of the area, the brunt of January precipitation fell on both the first and last days of the month, with very little in between. Thanks to not only a dry December and January, but also a predominantly dry November, many places within South Central Nebraska recorded one of the Top-5 driest 86-day stretches on record between the dates of Nov. 6-Jan. 30, including Grand Island airport, Kearney, Hastings, Osceola, Hebron and Holdrege. Specifically at Grand Island airport, it was the 3rd-driest Nov. 6-Jan. 30 stretch on record totaling only 0.33, and was the driest since 1980-1981. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the leanest January precipitation totals included 0.01 at Red Cloud, 0.04 at Hubbell, 0.05 at Hebron and 0.06 at Ord airport, Greeley and three miles northeast of Shelby. On the somewhat wetter side of things were stations located two miles northeast of Upland (0.43) and at Hastings airport and Clay Center (0.37). At the other official Tri-Cities airport sites, January precipitation totals featured 0.32 at Grand Island and 0.28 at Kearney. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of January, state-averaged soil moisture supplies clearly declined during the month. Topsoil moisture degraded notably since late-December, as the short to very short category increased from 33% to 60%, and the adequate category dropped to 40%. Subsoil moisture also degraded during the month but not quite as severely, increasing from 44% to 56% short to very short, while the adequate category decreased to 44%.

As often occurs to the northwest of a well-organized low pressure system, the primary north-south oriented snow band focused within an area of enhanced low to mid level frontogenesis/deformation.

In Greeley, the cooperative observer reported 2.3 inches of snowfall. This snow was combined with gusts from the north northwest up to around 40 mph, which reduced visibility. Some light snow and flurries occurred into the afternoon, but the bulk of accumulating snow was done by 8 am CST. A number of schools started late.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon through the early morning on the following day, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall setup along the Kansas-Nebraska border, stretching west into the foothills of the Rockies. The band intensified during the evening and lasted through almost sunrise before dissipating. There was almost no wind with this snowfall, which is rare for this area. Total snowfall accumulations for extreme Southwest Nebraska ranged from 14 at Haigler to 3 near Palisade.  The higher snowfall amounts were found in southwest Dundy county, declining to the east and north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 14 at Haigler to 6 5 WNW of Max.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system dropped southeast from Manitoba, Canada into the Great Lakes on this Thursday. As the low moved into the Great Lakes, its forward progress slowed considerably. The upper-level flow was highly amplified and it was approaching the axis of the upper-level trough. The slowing resulted in a very tight pressure gradient. At noon, the central pressure of the low was 993 mb over Lake Superior, with the pressure of the high 1040 mb over Idaho. The result was an extremely windy day, with the strongest winds occurring from late morning to late afternoon. Peak sustained winds were above 40 mph for at least one hour across the region. At Hastings, Grand Island, and Ord, winds were sustained at more than 40 mph for up to 4 hours. The highest peak wind gusts were mostly between 55 and 65 mph. The highest peak gusts included 65 mph at Ohiowa, in Fillmore county, and 73 mph at Norman, in Kearney county. Four small grass fires occurred across South Central Nebraska as well. Blowing dust was widespread, with the visibility as low as 2 miles in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated stations from surrounding counties measured sustained speeds of 40 MPH or higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the third is a series of four systems that produced very winds conditions, roughly every other day, over the seven-day period from January 14th to the 20th. The first event on the 14th was an advisory event. The second on the 16th was a warning event. The event on this Saturday was primarily an advisory event, but a few high wind gusts of 58 mph or greater did occur as a result of an Alberta Clipper that dove from the Canadian Prairies southeastward into Minnesota, Iowa, and eventually Illinois by evening. These strong winds occurred behind the cold front, which moved through around daybreak. Sustained winds of 30-39 mph were widespread, along with gusts of 45-55 mph. Lexington, Kearney, and Aurora did measure sustained winds of at least 40 mph. However, only Aurora satisfied the 1 hour criterion. A few locations managed to record gusts of 58 mph or more. The highest wind gust was 65 mph, measured at Ohiowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet site located 9 miles north-northeast of Lexington.  Time is an estimation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system tracked northeast into the central Great Plains.  It produced heavy snowfall and gusty northerly winds up to 40 mph throughout much of the southern Nebraska Panhandle.  The combination of falling and blowing snow created near-blizzard conditions and closed several roadways, including Interstate 80 from the Wyoming border east through Sidney.  Snowfall totals ranged from six to 14 inches.  Snow drifts varied between two and four feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was reported at Bridgeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure built down across the central plains on January 22nd and 23rd.   The high brought northwest winds of winds of 15 to 25 mph with higher gusts to the region.  Temperatures across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa fell into the single digits on the 22nd and into the 5 below to 10 below range over most of the area by the morning of the 23rd.  The combination of the cold and strong winds lowered wind chill values down into the 20 below to 30 below zero range.

An upper level storm system moved across the northern Plains on the morning of February 25th. This storm system moved along a strong temperature gradient over the central and northern Plains producing a band of accumulating snowfall over northeast Nebraska. The snow started falling during the morning hours and periods of snow, moderate at times, fell through the day ending during the evening on the 24th. Little wind occurred during the snow limiting impacts to mainly travel inconvenience. Total snowfall from the event ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general snowfall of 2 to 4 inches occurred across the county including 2.3 inches in Pierce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system moved out of the desert southwest and into the central Plains during Tuesday February 4th, and exited the region early on the morning on the 5th. As this storm system moved toward the area, low pressure developed over the southern Plains and moved into the mid Mississippi Valley. To the north of the low pressure, snow spread north into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the morning on the 4th and continued into the early morning hours on the 5th. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the late morning and into the mid afternoon hours across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska. As the low moved off to the east during the afternoon, winds became north and increased to 15 to 25 mph with gusts over 30 mph. This created blowing and drifting snow with some drifts up to two feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP reports were generally between 5 and 6 inches across the the county. This included 5 inches in Auburn and 6.2 inches near Nemaha. North winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 35 mph created considerable blowing and drifting snow, especially in open areas making travel difficult.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure pushed down into the central U.S. on January 5th and 6th bringing wind chill values of 25 below to 35 below zero or colder from the afternoon of the 5th through the afternoon of the 6th over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Actual temperatures across the area fell into the single digits above zero during the afternoon of January 5th and by the morning of January 6th they fell to around 10 below or colder.|Readings only recovered back into the single digits by the afternoon of the 6th.  With the initial Arctic surge during the afternoon of January 5th, northwest winds reached 25 to 35 mph with gusts into the 40s.  The winds decreased overnight, and actually dropped off to around 10 mph for a while toward sunrise.  However, winds picked back up into the 15 to 25 mph range during the daylight hours of January 6th before decreasing later that afternoon.

A powerful storm system moved from the northern Plains on the morning of the 16th to the mid Mississippi River Valley by the evening. Behind this system, across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa strong northwest developed during the morning and peaked in intensity during the early afternoon. Many locations across northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa recorded wind gusts greater than 60 mph with sustained winds greater than 40 mph during this time. The strong winds created some minor damage, power outages and blowing dust. Winds diminished during the evening hours on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some light sign damage was reported by the media in Beatrice from the storm winds including a Pizza Hut Sign.

To the north of the low pressure, snow spread north into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the morning on the 4th and continued into the early morning hours on the 5th. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the late morning and into the mid afternoon hours across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska. As the low moved off to the east during the afternoon, winds became north and increased to 15 to 25 mph with gusts over 30 mph. This created blowing and drifting snow with some drifts up to two feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper reports indicated several inches of snow were common across the county. The combination of the snow, cold and blowing snow led to school closings and difficult travel. North winds of 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 35 mph created considerable blowing and drifting snow, especially in open areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure pushed down into the central U.S. on January 5th and 6th bringing wind chill values of 25 below to 35 below zero or colder from the afternoon of the 5th through the afternoon of the 6th over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Actual temperatures across the area fell into the single digits above zero during the afternoon of January 5th and by the morning of January 6th they fell to around 10 below or colder.|Readings only recovered back into the single digits by the afternoon of the 6th.  With the initial Arctic surge during the afternoon of January 5th, northwest winds reached 25 to 35 mph with gusts into the 40s.  The winds decreased overnight, and actually dropped off to around 10 mph for a while toward sunrise.  However, winds picked back up into the 15 to 25 mph range during the daylight hours of January 6th before decreasing later that afternoon.

A powerful storm system moved from the northern Plains on the morning of the 16th to the mid Mississippi River Valley by the evening. Behind this system, across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa strong northwest developed during the morning and peaked in intensity during the early afternoon. Many locations across northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa recorded wind gusts greater than 60 mph with sustained winds greater than 40 mph during this time. The strong winds created some minor damage, power outages and blowing dust. Winds diminished during the evening hours on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimate based on surrounding observing equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system tracked northeast into the central Great Plains.  It produced heavy snowfall and gusty northerly winds up to 40 mph throughout much of the southern Nebraska Panhandle.  The combination of falling and blowing snow created near-blizzard conditions and closed several roadways, including Interstate 80 from the Wyoming border east through Sidney.  Snowfall totals ranged from six to 14 inches.  Snow drifts varied between two and four feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of snow was reported at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system intensified as it tracked northeast from the southern Rockies into the central Great Plains.  Light to moderate snowfall and northerly winds gusting to 35 mph created hazardous travel throughout the northern Nebraska Panhandle.  Snowfall totals ranged from three to six inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 13 miles south-southeast of Harrison estimated three inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level storm system moved into the Great Lakes during Sunday January 26th, an arctic cold front swept through the central Plains. The cold front brought widespread blizzard conditions to parts of the northern Plains into the Upper Mississippi River Valley, but farther west, where snow cover was lacking, just strong north winds were experienced across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska. The winds peaked during the late afternoon and early evening across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts estimated from surrounding observation sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across the northern Plains on the morning of February 25th. This storm system moved along a strong temperature gradient over the central and northern Plains producing a band of accumulating snowfall over northeast Nebraska. The snow started falling during the morning hours and periods of snow, moderate at times, fell through the day ending during the evening on the 24th. Little wind occurred during the snow limiting impacts to mainly travel inconvenience. Total snowfall from the event ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general snowfall of 3 to 6 inches occurred across the county.

Arctic high pressure built down across the central plains on January 22nd and 23rd.   The high brought northwest winds of winds of 15 to 25 mph with higher gusts to the region.  Temperatures across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa fell into the single digits on the 22nd and into the 5 below to 10 below range over most of the area by the morning of the 23rd.  The combination of the cold and strong winds lowered wind chill values down into the 20 below to 30 below zero range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure pushed down into the central U.S. on January 5th and 6th bringing wind chill values of 25 below to 35 below zero or colder from the afternoon of the 5th through the afternoon of the 6th over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Actual temperatures across the area fell into the single digits above zero during the afternoon of January 5th and by the morning of January 6th they fell to around 10 below or colder.|Readings only recovered back into the single digits by the afternoon of the 6th.  With the initial Arctic surge during the afternoon of January 5th, northwest winds reached 25 to 35 mph with gusts into the 40s.  The winds decreased overnight, and actually dropped off to around 10 mph for a while toward sunrise.  However, winds picked back up into the 15 to 25 mph range during the daylight hours of January 6th before decreasing later that afternoon.

A powerful storm system moved from the northern Plains on the morning of the 16th to the mid Mississippi River Valley by the evening. Behind this system, across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa strong northwest developed during the morning and peaked in intensity during the early afternoon. Many locations across northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa recorded wind gusts greater than 60 mph with sustained winds greater than 40 mph during this time. The strong winds created some minor damage, power outages and blowing dust. Winds diminished during the evening hours on the 16th.

Arctic high pressure built down across the central plains on January 22nd and 23rd.   The high brought northwest winds of winds of 15 to 25 mph with higher gusts to the region.  Temperatures across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa fell into the single digits on the 22nd and into the 5 below to 10 below range over most of the area by the morning of the 23rd.  The combination of the cold and strong winds lowered wind chill values down into the 20 below to 30 below zero range.

A potent storm system moved out of the desert southwest and into the central Plains during Tuesday February 4th, and exited the region early on the morning on the 5th. As this storm system moved toward the area, low pressure developed over the southern Plains and moved into the mid Mississippi Valley. To the north of the low pressure, snow spread north into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the morning on the 4th and continued into the early morning hours on the 5th. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the late morning and into the mid afternoon hours across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska. As the low moved off to the east during the afternoon, winds became north and increased to 15 to 25 mph with gusts over 30 mph. This created blowing and drifting snow with some drifts up to two feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 5 inches were reported in the county. This included 3.4 inches in Gretna, 4 inches at Offutt Air Force Base, and 4.6 inches in Papillion. This combined with north winds of 15 to 30 mph with gusts to 35 mph created considerable blowing and drifting snow, especially in open areas. The snow and winds made travel difficult.

A low pressure trough over northern Nebraska produced a brief period of moderate to heavy snow.  Gusty north winds of 35 to 45 mph produced considerable blowing snow and very low visibilities across portions of the northern Nebraska Panhandle.  Snow accumulations from six to twelve inches, heaviest along the Pine Ridge.  One to three foot snow drifts, closed several county and state roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from three to six inches, higher along the Pine Ridge.

After sunset wind chills below -25 returned to Red Willow county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving but potent storm system moved south from the eastern Dakota into Iowa during the day on the 18th. A cold front moved rapidly south across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa in the early morning. Behind the cold front, winds increased from the northwest across the area during the morning as high pressure built into the area. Northwest sustained winds of 20 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph were common, but isolated locations recorded higher winds greater than 58 mph with some light damage reported. The winds decreased by late afternoon as the storm system pushed off to the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system moved out of the desert southwest and into the central Plains during Tuesday February 4th, and exited the region early on the morning on the 5th. As this storm system moved toward the area, low pressure developed over the southern Plains and moved into the mid Mississippi Valley. To the north of the low pressure, snow spread north into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the morning on the 4th and continued into the early morning hours on the 5th. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the late morning and into the mid afternoon hours across western Iowa and eastern Nebraska. As the low moved off to the east during the afternoon, winds became north and increased to 15 to 25 mph with gusts over 30 mph. This created blowing and drifting snow with some drifts up to two feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP reports were generally between 5 and 6 inches across the  central and southern two third of the county. This included 6.1 inches in Virginia and 5.1 inches at Beatrice.  Considerable blowing and drifting snow was reported in open areas making travel difficult.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms develop along a cold front across northeast Nebraska during the afternoon on June 28th. The majority of these storms were not severe, but isolated reports of large hail was received from the storms. The thunderstorms decreased in coverage and intensity by late evening.

A band of 2 to more than 3 inches of rain fell across the Logan Creek basin during the 24 hours ending June 15th.  The heavy rain caused minor flooding along Logan Creek from near Pender through Uehling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Logan Creek to climb slightly above it's 18 foot flood stage around the Uehling area from the afternoon of June 15 into the morning hours of the 16th.  The flooding mainly impacted some farmland and a few roads near the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy rain along the Pebble Creek basin caused that creek to flood for the second time in around a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of 2 to 3 inches of rain fell across the Pebble Creek basin causing it to flood for the second time in around a week.  The creek near Scribner reached a crest around 22.3 feet during the afternoon of June 27th, flood stage is 18 feet.  The flooding impacted county roads and farmland near the stream.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow was reported, heaviest southeast of Bridgeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through January 2014 across much of western Nebraska. Precipitation was sparse during the month that led to continued Severe Drought (D2) conditions with Extreme Drought (D3) conditions in parts of six counties, the most extensive in Chase County. Precipitation ranged from less than a half-inch across the north to local areas up to one inch in the southwest in the form of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through January 2014. Severe Drought (D2) conditions lingered as precipitation in the form of snow produced a half inch or less of moisture in the county. The drought continued into February 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought rain, followed by freezing rain, sleet and snow to portions of western and north central Nebraska. The storm began as rain early in the afternoon on December 14th, and transitioned to freezing rain, sleet, then snow, during the overnight hours and daytime hours on December 15th.  The heaviest snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 9 inches across north central Nebraska. Freezing rain and sleet caused additional impacts across eastern portions of north central Nebraska, causing very icy roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General public near Hay Springs and Gordon both reported 6 inches of snowfall.  North winds of 20 to 30 mph caused blowing and drifting with low visibility during the overnight hours into the morning hours on December 15th.

The drought that began in June 2012 continued through January 2014 across much of western Nebraska. Precipitation was sparse during the month that led to continued Severe Drought (D2) conditions with Extreme Drought (D3) conditions in parts of six counties, the most extensive in Chase County. Precipitation ranged from less than a half-inch across the north to local areas up to one inch in the southwest in the form of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through January 2014. Severe Drought (D2) conditions lingered as precipitation in the form of snow produced around a half inch of moisture in the county. The drought continued into February 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from repeated thunderstorms in late September caused a number of rivers in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa to go into flood. Much of the flooding was minor, but flooding along the Salt Creek and associated tributaries was more significant and in some locations exceeded 100 and 200 year return intervals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of September caused the Turkey Creek to go into flood. The river rose above flood stage in the early morning on the 1st, crested in the early afternoon on the 1st, and fell below flood stage in the morning of the 2nd. The river flooding was minor and mainly confined to low-lying areas along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from repeated thunderstorms in late September caused a number of rivers in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa to go into flood. Much of the flooding was minor, but flooding along the Salt Creek and associated tributaries was more significant and in some locations exceeded 100 and 200 year return intervals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of September caused the Salt Creek and many of it's tributaries to go into flood. The flooding included the main stem of the Salt Creek, Little Salt Creek, and Stevens Creek. The creek is a fast response creek and rose above flood stage early in the morning on the 1st, crested in most locations during the early morning hours and fell below flood stage by afternoon or early evening. Most of the flooding along the system was minor, but the flooding along the main stem of the Salt Creek at Greenwood reached major stage. The flooding along the system was at a return interval of 100 years, and in some spots it was a 200 year flood. Numerous roads were closed due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barn was reported blown down, as well as several trees including at least one 2 feet in diameter.

Considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities created significant impacts to holiday travel across much of the western Nebraska Panhandle. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved across Kansas and into Iowa during the day on the 15th. Although the system largely was lacking in cold air, sufficiently cold air was able to move into place to allow the rain to mix with sleet and freezing rain early in the morning on the 15th, eventually becoming all snow after sunrise. The snow continued into the afternoon on the 15th before tapering to flurries by the evening. As the surface low moved into Iowa during the evening northwest winds did increase allowing for some blowing and drifting of the new snowfall, but this was limited by the wet nature of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports from COOP observers across Knox County, as well as local law enforcement indicated that 5 to 6 inches of snowfall was widespread across the county. Specific snowfall amounts included 5.4 inches in Verdel, 4.9 inches in Bloomfield, and 5.5 inches in Crofton. Freezing rain and sleet were also reported, but ice accumulations were generally minor. Northwest winds increased during the afternoon on the 15th with gusts over 40 mph. Although the snowfall was wet, some blowing and drifting of the new snowfall did occur.

A cold front moved through Southwest Nebraska during the early morning.  Strong winds behind the initial frontal passage caused blowing dust to develop as the front moved through, causing visibilities to fall to less than a quarter mile briefly.  Behind the front stronger winds around 60 MPH developed creating even more blowing dust.  The worst of the blowing dust was of Hitchcock and Red Willow counties.  Satellite imagery showed the dust plumes originating over Hitchcock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The blowing dust reduced visibilities to less than a quarter mile across the county around sunrise with the initial frontal passage then again around nine in the morning with the start of the high winds.

High pressure over the southern plains brought dry, windy and relatively warm conditions to eastern Nebraska on January 29th.  Afternoon temperatures climbed into the 40s as relative humidity values dropped into the 20 to 30 percent range as south winds gusted between 20 and 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire burned a little over 300 acres around Lake Cunningham on the far northwest outskirts of Omaha.  Lack of precipitation and snow cover much of January helped cause very dry conditions, and gusty south winds and low relative humidity values fed the flames which climbed as high as 5 to 10 feet.  Although no damage was caused other than vegetation, the fire took a little over 6 hours to extinguish.

A seasonably strong shortwave trough moved into the northern Plains during the day on Monday July 7th. This allowed for a cold front to advance southeast across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening hours. Due to persistent clouds, as well as frontal passage the previous day resulting in limited moisture, the atmosphere was only moderately unstable for July standards. A band of elevated showers and thunderstorms slowly intensified as they moved southeast across the area. The thunderstorms become the most intense over far southwest Iowa and southeast Nebraska closer to a stalled frontal boundary. The thunderstorms quickly moved into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri by late evening ending the severe weather threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated winds between 50 and 60 mph.

Northwest flow continued to bring disturbances southeast on the 9th. Scattered thunderstorms produced numerous large hail events, the largest baseball size. The combination of hail and winds stripped some crops in Custer County and produced storm wind damage. The tropical plume of moisture maintained a heavy rain threat as the storms moved southeast that produced secondary road washouts in Holt County.

A seasonably strong shortwave trough moved into the northern Plains during the day on Monday July 7th. This allowed for a cold front to advance southeast across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening hours. Due to persistent clouds, as well as frontal passage the previous day resulting in limited moisture, the atmosphere was only moderately unstable for July standards. A band of elevated showers and thunderstorms slowly intensified as they moved southeast across the area. The thunderstorms become the most intense over far southwest Iowa and southeast Nebraska closer to a stalled frontal boundary. The thunderstorms quickly moved into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri by late evening ending the severe weather threat.

Thunderstorms developed shortly after midnight over eastern Nebraska and moved east into western Iowa. Marginal instability and shear limited the overall severe weather threat from these thunderstorms, but deep moisture, repeated thunderstorms, and a slow movement to the complex, heavy rain was widespread across far northeast Nebraska and western Iowa. General rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches, but isolated locations received between 4 and 5 inches of rainfall. This lead to some localized flash flooding as well as river flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed within a warm air advection pattern during the late night over eastern South Dakota. A broken line of supercells then moved into northeast Nebraska by mid morning and continued southward to near the Interstate 80 corridor before dissipating in the mid afternoon. These thunderstorms were responsible for large hail and isolated reports of damaging winds, mainly in northeast Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed within a warm air advection pattern during the late night over eastern South Dakota. These thunderstorms moved southeast into better instability and shear over far southeast South Dakota and intensified. A broken line of supercells then moved into northeast Nebraska by mid morning and continued southward to near the Interstate 80 corridor before dissipating in the mid afternoon. These thunderstorms were responsible for large hail and isolated reports of damaging winds, mainly in northeast Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong shortwave trough moved into the northern Plains during the day on Monday July 7th. This allowed for a cold front to advance southeast across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening hours. Due to persistent clouds, as well as frontal passage the previous day resulting in limited moisture, the atmosphere was only moderately unstable for July standards. A band of elevated showers and thunderstorms slowly intensified as they moved southeast across the area. The thunderstorms become the most intense over far southwest Iowa and southeast Nebraska closer to a stalled frontal boundary. The thunderstorms quickly moved into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri by late evening ending the severe weather threat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several heavy rain events in June and early July brought continued or renewed flooding along the Missouri River from around Brownville downstream through Rulo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain events in late June and early July brought prolonged flooding along the Missouri River from around Brownville through Rulo.  At Rulo the river crested around 18.5 feet on July 3rd, fell briefly below its 17 foot flood stage on the 4th and 5th and then reached a second crest around 18 feet late on the 6th.  The river fell below flood stage midday on the 7th.  The flooding impacted a few county roads and cabins along the River.
