During the late evening hours of Wednesday August 20th, scattered thunderstorms moved into portions of South Central Nebraska from the west. Just after midnight on Thursday the 21st, a possible cell merger resulted in the intensification of a storm over southeastern Buffalo and extreme northern Kearney counties. This storm produced some pockets of wind damage from these areas eastward to near the town of Prosser in northern Adams county, where it continued moving east just south of Interstate 80. Large tree branches were knocked down, a pole barn was damaged and an irrigation pivot was overturned. Much later in the day, during the early evening hours, an arc of thunderstorms developed over North Central Kansas. These storms expanded northward into portions of South Central Nebraska, apparently initiated by an outflow boundary emanating from the initial storms. One storm over York County was strong enough to produce one inch hail.||These storms formed in the vicinity of a wavy frontal boundary that was quasi-stationary which gradually became mobile and advanced east across Nebraska during the daytime hours. The longwave pattern was typical of summer, with a subtropical high over the Gulf Coast states. The flow was low amplitude with a general ridge over the eastern U.S. and a trough over the west. The thermodynamic and momentum environments did change from the overnight hours into the early evening hours. Overnight, mid-level lapse rates were steep (7.5 degrees C/km), resulting in MUCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg. And while 40 knots of upper-level flow existed, the low-level jet of similar magnitude resulted in poor deep layer shear under 30 knots. By early evening, MLCAPE had increased to between 2000 and 3000 J/kg, with mid-level lapse rates a little more marginal, near 7 degrees C/km. Deep layer shear had increased to 30-35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 70 MPH. Multiple tree branches were downed, some as large as 6 inches in diameter.  A small fruit tree was also uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This late Monday evening the 25th into early Tuesday morning the 26th featured one of the most noteworthy, albeit very-localized heavy rain and flooding events of summer 2014 within South Central Nebraska, with the greatest concentration of 3+ inch amounts centered directly over and near the city of York. The vast majority of this rain fell during a three-hour period between 1130 pm-230 am CDT, with a few of the highest rain totals in the immediate York area featuring 6.40 on the southwest side of town (NeRAIN), 4.93 on the southeast side (NeRAIN) and 4.71 inches a few miles north of the city (NWS coop observer). As a result of this deluge, significant street flooding temporarily stranded several motorists during the night and Beaver Creek remained out of its banks well into the Tuesday daytime hours within the city. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall left some drivers stranded as flood waters spilled into the streets. York police reported that a number of motorists were stranded in their cars after trying to drive through high water. Rain flooded an area between Lincoln Ave. and Platte Ave. from 2nd to 6th streets. Beaver Creek, which runs through the center of York, was also well over its banks early Tuesday morning. Reports of submerged cars were also received via Facebook. A total of 6.4 inches of rain was measured about one mile southwest of York from an NERAIN report. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line swept across South Central Nebraska on this Friday evening, but remained mostly below severe limits. Just before 5 p.m. CDT, an isolated supercell formed over Phillips County, Kansas. This storm split and the left-mover advanced northwestward into Harlan and Furnas counties. This storm briefly produced hail up to the size of nickels near Oxford, then rapidly dissipated thereafter. Meanwhile, scattered thunderstorms over central Kansas formed a line along a common cold pool. A squall line formed and advanced north-northeastward across South Central Nebraska between 8 p.m. and midnight CDT. This line produced multiple wind gusts above 50 MPH, including one measured gust of 58 MPH at a mesonet site in the town of Pleasanton.||A quasi-stationary front extended from southwest-northeast across Kansas and Nebraska, serving as the focus for the thunderstorm development. The upper level flow was characterized by a broad anticyclonic circulation over the Mississippi Valley with a trough over the western states. Prior to the storms moving in, temperatures were in the lower to middle 80s with dewpoints in the lower to middle 70s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, but the high dewpoints resulted in MLCAPE around 3,000 J/kg. The polar jet stream was just west of the region and arced anticyclonically through the Northern Plains. Deep layer shear was just under 30 knots which was favorable for primarily multicell storm structures. However, a little earlier during the late afternoon hours, winds were backed enough to result in deep layer shear of 35 knots and the lone supercell did develop within this stronger shear environment.

During the late evening hours of Wednesday August 20th, scattered thunderstorms moved into portions of South Central Nebraska from the west. Just after midnight on Thursday the 21st, a possible cell merger resulted in the intensification of a storm over southeastern Buffalo and extreme northern Kearney counties. This storm produced some pockets of wind damage from these areas eastward to near the town of Prosser in northern Adams county, where it continued moving east just south of Interstate 80. Large tree branches were knocked down, a pole barn was damaged and an irrigation pivot was overturned. Much later in the day, during the early evening hours, an arc of thunderstorms developed over North Central Kansas. These storms expanded northward into portions of South Central Nebraska, apparently initiated by an outflow boundary emanating from the initial storms. One storm over York County was strong enough to produce one inch hail.||These storms formed in the vicinity of a wavy frontal boundary that was quasi-stationary which gradually became mobile and advanced east across Nebraska during the daytime hours. The longwave pattern was typical of summer, with a subtropical high over the Gulf Coast states. The flow was low amplitude with a general ridge over the eastern U.S. and a trough over the west. The thermodynamic and momentum environments did change from the overnight hours into the early evening hours. Overnight, mid-level lapse rates were steep (7.5 degrees C/km), resulting in MUCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg. And while 40 knots of upper-level flow existed, the low-level jet of similar magnitude resulted in poor deep layer shear under 30 knots. By early evening, MLCAPE had increased to between 2000 and 3000 J/kg, with mid-level lapse rates a little more marginal, near 7 degrees C/km. Deep layer shear had increased to 30-35 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rounds of convection affected parts of South Central Nebraska on this Friday, one earlier in the day that produced locally heavy rainfall in far-eastern counties and another later in the afternoon that featured an isolated severe storm over Hall County. Starting with the later round, a small cluster of fairly weak storms started developing in northern portions of the area (primarily Greeley-Nance-Howard counties) at mid-day, slowly sinking southward. As time went by, afternoon heating/destabilization led to intensification along the southwest flank of this storm cluster, resulting in two small severe storms that merged together as they drifted southward through Hall County between 3 pm and 430 pm CDT. There were a few reports of quarter size hail along with estimated 60 MPH gusts just southwest of Grand Island, but this storm steadily weakened and dissipated as it crossed eastern Adams County. Later in the evening, a few more strong storms rumbled into northwestern counties such as Valley and Greeley, but they remained sub-severe. Turning back to earlier in the day, a rather narrow, north-south oriented line of non-severe storms initially blossomed near the Highway 81 corridor from Polk County southward into Thayer County between 730-900 am CDT and persisted for several hours before dissipating early in the afternoon. Though most affected areas received less than 2 inches of rain, a particularly intense and narrow band set up directly over Osceola where the NWS cooperative observer tallied 3.17, most of which fell in two hours between 830-1030 am CDT and promoted minor flooding in the area. ||In the mid and upper levels, both rounds of convection were sparked by the passage of a shortwave trough slowly shifting east from central Nebraska into western Iowa over the course of the day. The early round of heavy-rain producing storms near Highway 81 focused along a convergent zone in the heart of the trough axis, while the isolated severe storm during the afternoon occurred within a northwesterly-flow regime just behind the departing mid level trough axis and also along a weak surface trough.  Although the mesoscale environment was not overly-supportive of severe weather, the combination of mixed-layer CAPE around 1000 J/kg and fairly weak 0-6 kilometer deep layer shear up to 30 knots proved sufficient enough for the brief, isolated threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 60 mph were also reported with the quarter size hail. This was reported by the general public driving on Interstate 80 and was relayed by law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of scattered thunderstorms moved into Central Nebraska late this Saturday afternoon. One of these storms was severe and tracked across Valley, Sherman and Howard counties. This storm entered western Valley county just after 5 p.m. CDT and then tracked east along the Valley-Sherman county border. It then progressed across northern Howard County, rapidly weakening after 6 p.m. CDT. This storm produced one inch hail in multiple locations during that hour.||These storms formed in associated with a very weak surface low. The upper levels were characterized by a moderately amplified ridge over the western U.S. and a trough over the east. Prior to the storms moving in, temperatures were in the upper 80s F and dewpoints were in the lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were fairly steep, around 7.5 degrees C/km. However, the high surface dewpoint depression resulted MLCAPE values between 1500 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer wind shear (0-6 km) was 35 to 40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive night, widespread thunderstorm activity blossomed across most all of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area on this Saturday, although just like the previous night all severe-criteria hail and wind reports emanated from counties along and west of the Highway 281 corridor. Early in the evening, an isolated severe storm dropped torrential rainfall and hail up to golf ball size over central Franklin County. Thereafter, the majority of severe weather later in the evening was associated with a pair of southeastward-surging storm clusters. The western-most one peaked in intensity over Dawson and western Buffalo counties, highlighted by reports of quarter to golf ball size hail in the Elm Creek area and golf ball to baseball size hail in the Lexington area. A bit farther east and a bit later, a separate severe storm cluster was more of a damaging wind producer as it raced through southern Hall and Adams counties and portions of neighboring counties. In these areas, estimated 60 MPH winds were reported in the Ayr and Bladen areas, and more significant peak gusts of slightly over 70 MPH were measured near Wood River and at Hastings airport, resulting in reports of a few large tree limbs down in the city. Fortunately for the majority of South Central Nebraska, most storms on this night produced only sub-severe wind gusts and non-flooding rainfall mainly under 1.50 inches. Localized pockets of the area measured higher rainfall amounts, but none more so than a small zone of very heavy rain up to around 6 that fell in east-central Franklin County, most of which from the aforementioned, slow-moving early evening storm. ||Breaking down the timing of thunderstorm evolution in more detail, it's worth noting that well-before the onset of the evening main event several strong thunderstorms rumbled across the far northern/northeastern fringes of the local area for much of the day. Although radar suggested a few of these daytime storms may have produced larger hail (especially in northern Valley/Greeley counties), limited ground-truth reports yielded no larger than dime size stones. The primary evening event got underway between 630-730 pm CDT as a slow-moving, isolated severe storm blew up over Franklin County before eventually departing southeastward into northern Kansas. As the Franklin County storm was in progress, a southeastward-surging supercell cluster entered the local area from Custer County between 730-830 pm CDT, primarily impacting Dawson County and vicinity. Over the next few hours, this initial severe storm cluster weakened while a plethora of additional multicell and linear thunderstorm complexes blossomed over the majority of South Central Nebraska. While much of this activity was sub-severe, a second southeast-surging storm complex produced the damaging winds in the Hall/Adams counties area between 10-11 pm CDT. After 11 pm CDT, all storms in South Central Nebraska exhibited a steady weakening trend although widespread showers and embedded thunderstorms lingered well past midnight in many areas. ||This was a fairly typical late-summer severe weather event featuring fairly high instability values but only modest deep layer wind shear and weak large scale forcing. In the mid to upper levels, generally quasi-zonal flow was in place over the Central Plains, containing one or more fairly subtle shortwave troughs. At the surface, storms during the main evening event initiated in the vicinity of a northwest-southeast oriented quasi stationary front draped from western Nebraska into north central Kansas, with this front partially composed of a remnant outflow boundary from the previous night's convection. With surface dewpoints well into the mid-60s to low-70s F across the local area, most-unstable CAPE climbed as high as 2000-3000 J/kg. Although a few storms briefly exhibited supercell structures, 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear only averaging around 30 knots promoted a predominant multicell/quasi-linear mode.

Thunderstorms developed over South Dakota and northwest Nebraska, then pushed southeast through northern and central Nebraska. These storms intensified to produce very heavy rain, hail, and strong winds. Hail up to golf ball size was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell at a residence 17 miles NW of Mullen, NE.

Localized pockets of the area measured higher rainfall amounts, but none more so than a small zone of very heavy rain up to around 6 that fell in east-central Franklin County, most of which from the aforementioned, slow-moving early evening storm. ||Breaking down the timing of thunderstorm evolution in more detail, it's worth noting that well-before the onset of the evening main event several strong thunderstorms rumbled across the far northern/northeastern fringes of the local area for much of the day. Although radar suggested a few of these daytime storms may have produced larger hail (especially in northern Valley/Greeley counties), limited ground-truth reports yielded no larger than dime size stones. The primary evening event got underway between 630-730 pm CDT as a slow-moving, isolated severe storm blew up over Franklin County before eventually departing southeastward into northern Kansas. As the Franklin County storm was in progress, a southeastward-surging supercell cluster entered the local area from Custer County between 730-830 pm CDT, primarily impacting Dawson County and vicinity. Over the next few hours, this initial severe storm cluster weakened while a plethora of additional multicell and linear thunderstorm complexes blossomed over the majority of South Central Nebraska. While much of this activity was sub-severe, a second southeast-surging storm complex produced the damaging winds in the Hall/Adams counties area between 10-11 pm CDT. After 11 pm CDT, all storms in South Central Nebraska exhibited a steady weakening trend although widespread showers and embedded thunderstorms lingered well past midnight in many areas. ||This was a fairly typical late-summer severe weather event featuring fairly high instability values but only modest deep layer wind shear and weak large scale forcing. In the mid to upper levels, generally quasi-zonal flow was in place over the Central Plains, containing one or more fairly subtle shortwave troughs. At the surface, storms during the main evening event initiated in the vicinity of a northwest-southeast oriented quasi stationary front draped from western Nebraska into north central Kansas, with this front partially composed of a remnant outflow boundary from the previous night's convection. With surface dewpoints well into the mid-60s to low-70s F across the local area, most-unstable CAPE climbed as high as 2000-3000 J/kg. Although a few storms briefly exhibited supercell structures, 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear only averaging around 30 knots promoted a predominant multicell/quasi-linear mode.

Thereafter, the majority of severe weather later in the evening was associated with a pair of southeastward-surging storm clusters. The western-most one peaked in intensity over Dawson and western Buffalo counties, highlighted by reports of quarter to golf ball size hail in the Elm Creek area and golf ball to baseball size hail in the Lexington area. Just before 2 a.m. CDT on this Wednesday, a severe thunderstorm rapidly formed over southern Gosper County. This storm became supercellular as it slowly propagated southeastward into Furnas and Harlan counties. The supercell split between 2:30 and 3:30 a.m. CDT, with the left-mover propagating eastward across northern Harlan County. While it was difficult to obtain severe weather reports given the time of night, severe hail was reported, with the largest the size of baseballs in the town of Ragan. This hail was associated with the left-moving storm. However, radar data suggested that very large hail may also have occurred with the right-mover over sparsely populated portions of eastern Furnas County. The slow movement of the right-mover, combined with another weaker storm that followed it, resulted in a swath of very heavy rainfall of 2 and 3 inches. Other scattered single cell and multicell thunderstorms developed around this supercell activity. All of these storms and their remnants continued southeast and dissipated around sunrise. In the meantime, another cluster of strong thunderstorms was moving southeast from the North Platte area. This cluster weakened as it moved into Gosper, Furnas and Harlan counties around mid-morning, but not before producing 1 inch hail in the town of Arapahoe.||These storms formed north of a cold front that slipped south through the region the previous morning. The forcing for these thunderstorms was extremely subtle and not readily identifiable. Hot subtropical high pressure was over the western two-thirds of the country, centered over New Mexico. In fact, based on the evening radiosonde launch at Grand Junction, Colorado, the observed 500 mb height was 6000 m (above sea level). The associated ridge axis extended north through Wyoming and Montana, resulting in northwest flow over the Central Plains. Underneath, the flow was easterly from the surface up to 800 mb. So, the most probable reason for convective initiation was upslope flow. Warm air advection and frontogenesis were non-existent. While the inflow region could not be determined, the enlarged hodographs suggested low level and deep layer shear were sufficient for supercell storm structures. MUCAPE was estimated between 1000 and 2000 J/kg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell late in the evening, while hail remained on the ground in the morning. Thunderstorm winds also caused some broken tree branches in the area.

This late Monday evening the 25th into early Tuesday morning the 26th featured one of the most noteworthy, albeit very-localized heavy rain and flooding events of summer 2014 within South Central Nebraska, with the greatest concentration of 3+ inch amounts centered directly over and near the city of York. The vast majority of this rain fell during a three-hour period between 1130 pm-230 am CDT, with a few of the highest rain totals in the immediate York area featuring 6.40 on the southwest side of town (NeRAIN), 4.93 on the southeast side (NeRAIN) and 4.71 inches a few miles north of the city (NWS coop observer). As a result of this deluge, significant street flooding temporarily stranded several motorists during the night and Beaver Creek remained out of its banks well into the Tuesday daytime hours within the city. Although flooding in central York County was the main story on this night, hail up to quarter size was also reported in York, and farther west, penny to nickel size hail fell in Hastings and Phillips. ||This event was triggered by the rapid 11 pm-12 am CDT initiation of a west-southwest to east-northeast corridor of elevated convection that started out no more than 10 to 25 miles wide, and extended from the Minden and Hastings areas in the west and then eastward through the Aurora and York areas. While this primary area of convection steadily ended from west-to-east during the next few hours as it expanded in area somewhat, the York area bore the brunt of heavy rainfall as it not only received heavy rain from the initial development, but also had to endure training convection moving in from the west. Although small pockets of weak thunderstorms lingered over South Central Nebraska throughout the night, the main heavy rain event wrapped up between 130-230 am CDT. ||This was a fairly classic case of a narrow corridor of elevated nocturnal thunderstorms blossoming within the exit region of a modestly-strengthening low level jet centered near/slightly above 850 millibars. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided with a zone of slightly enhanced southwesterly flow focused well-downstream from a positively tilted western United States trough centered over Nevada. At the surface, South Central Nebraska resided north of a generally west-east quasi-stationary front draped across Kansas. Around the time of late-evening convective initiation, mesoscale parameters featured around 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear around 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few hail stones were as large as pennies, but most were smaller, in east central Hastings.

During a four-hour period centered from 630-1030 pm CDT on this Sunday evening, a complex of severe thunderstorms tracked from northwest-to-southeast through primarily the southwestern two-thirds of South Central Nebraska, resulting in numerous reports of damaging winds ranging from 60 to around 80 MPH. Hail was not a significant issue, with nickel size stones being the largest reported. Although high winds also affected some counties farther west, the primary swath of damaging winds was roughly 20-40 miles wide, extending from the Valley/Sherman counties area in the northwest, southeastward through the Hall/Adams/Nuckolls counties areas. Along this main corridor, a sampling of the most wind significant reports in chronological order included: estimated 70 MPH winds in the Elyria and Litchfield areas with crop and tree damage, a measured 71 MPH gust near Wood River, a measured peak gust of 83 MPH at Hastings airport along with 10-20 minutes of persistent 60-80 MPH wind behind the initial surge, and up to 30 minutes of estimated 60-70 MPH gusts in Superior. Off to the west of this main zone of wind issues, there were a few reports of gusts around 60 MPH in the Lexington and Arapahoe areas. Rainfall-wise, the progressive nature of convection kept totals in most places no higher than 0.50-1.50 inches, with a few of the wetter exceptions per NeRAIN observes featuring 2.30 nine miles northwest of Ord and 2.13 six miles southeast of Davenport. ||In a basic sense, this event consisted of an initially semi-discrete supercell cluster that first developed in north central Nebraska during the mid-afternoon hours and then gradually grew upscale into a larger-scale, forward-propagating linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) as it crossed the local South Central Nebraska area. This intense storm complex first infiltrated the local area into Valley/Sherman/Dawson counties between 630-730 pm CDT, blew through the Grand Island/Hastings areas during the 815-930 pm CDT time frame and finally departed southeastward into northern Kansas between 10-11 pm CDT. Although showers/weak storms lingered over mainly eastern counties a bit longer, the vast majority of South Central Nebraska was void of precipitation by 1 am CDT on the 18th. ||By late-summer standards, this synoptic and mesoscale setup was fairly ripe for an organized severe weather event. In the mid-upper levels, a low-amplitude shortwave trough brushed into Nebraska from the northwest, along the southern periphery of a larger scale disturbance centered over southern Saskatchewan/Manitoba. As a result, mid-upper level flow increased enough to promote generally 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear over South Central Nebraska. In addition, instability was quite potent as the combination of afternoon surface temperatures in the upper-80s/near 90 F and dewpoints in the mid-60s/low-70s promoted mixed-layer CAPE values of 2000-4000 J/kg ahead of the incoming convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter measured the wind gust with a hand held anemometer.

Along this main corridor, a sampling of the most wind significant reports in chronological order included: estimated 70 MPH winds in the Elyria and Litchfield areas with crop and tree damage, a measured 71 MPH gust near Wood River, a measured peak gust of 83 MPH at Hastings airport along with 10-20 minutes of persistent 60-80 MPH wind behind the initial surge, and up to 30 minutes of estimated 60-70 MPH gusts in Superior. Off to the west of this main zone of wind issues, there were a few reports of gusts around 60 MPH in the Lexington and Arapahoe areas. Rainfall-wise, the progressive nature of convection kept totals in most places no higher than 0.50-1.50 inches, with a few of the wetter exceptions per NeRAIN observes featuring 2.30 nine miles northwest of Ord and 2.13 six miles southeast of Davenport. ||In a basic sense, this event consisted of an initially semi-discrete supercell cluster that first developed in north central Nebraska during the mid-afternoon hours and then gradually grew upscale into a larger-scale, forward-propagating linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) as it crossed the local South Central Nebraska area. This intense storm complex first infiltrated the local area into Valley/Sherman/Dawson counties between 630-730 pm CDT, blew through the Grand Island/Hastings areas during the 815-930 pm CDT time frame and finally departed southeastward into northern Kansas between 10-11 pm CDT. Although showers/weak storms lingered over mainly eastern counties a bit longer, the vast majority of South Central Nebraska was void of precipitation by 1 am CDT on the 18th. ||By late-summer standards, this synoptic and mesoscale setup was fairly ripe for an organized severe weather event. In the mid-upper levels, a low-amplitude shortwave trough brushed into Nebraska from the northwest, along the southern periphery of a larger scale disturbance centered over southern Saskatchewan/Manitoba. As a result, mid-upper level flow increased enough to promote generally 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear over South Central Nebraska. In addition, instability was quite potent as the combination of afternoon surface temperatures in the upper-80s/near 90 F and dewpoints in the mid-60s/low-70s promoted mixed-layer CAPE values of 2000-4000 J/kg ahead of the incoming convective complex.

This late Monday evening the 25th into early Tuesday morning the 26th featured one of the most noteworthy, albeit very-localized heavy rain and flooding events of summer 2014 within South Central Nebraska, with the greatest concentration of 3+ inch amounts centered directly over and near the city of York. The vast majority of this rain fell during a three-hour period between 1130 pm-230 am CDT, with a few of the highest rain totals in the immediate York area featuring 6.40 on the southwest side of town (NeRAIN), 4.93 on the southeast side (NeRAIN) and 4.71 inches a few miles north of the city (NWS coop observer). As a result of this deluge, significant street flooding temporarily stranded several motorists during the night and Beaver Creek remained out of its banks well into the Tuesday daytime hours within the city. Although flooding in central York County was the main story on this night, hail up to quarter size was also reported in York, and farther west, penny to nickel size hail fell in Hastings and Phillips. ||This event was triggered by the rapid 11 pm-12 am CDT initiation of a west-southwest to east-northeast corridor of elevated convection that started out no more than 10 to 25 miles wide, and extended from the Minden and Hastings areas in the west and then eastward through the Aurora and York areas. While this primary area of convection steadily ended from west-to-east during the next few hours as it expanded in area somewhat, the York area bore the brunt of heavy rainfall as it not only received heavy rain from the initial development, but also had to endure training convection moving in from the west. Although small pockets of weak thunderstorms lingered over South Central Nebraska throughout the night, the main heavy rain event wrapped up between 130-230 am CDT. ||This was a fairly classic case of a narrow corridor of elevated nocturnal thunderstorms blossoming within the exit region of a modestly-strengthening low level jet centered near/slightly above 850 millibars. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided with a zone of slightly enhanced southwesterly flow focused well-downstream from a positively tilted western United States trough centered over Nevada. At the surface, South Central Nebraska resided north of a generally west-east quasi-stationary front draped across Kansas. Around the time of late-evening convective initiation, mesoscale parameters featured around 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear around 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was received via Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late spring storm brought heavy snowfall amounts up to 10 inches tin Sheridan County. The heaviest snowfall amounts of 8 to 10 inches fell across north, where northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph reduced visibility to a quarter mile or less at times. Visibility varied as considerable blowing and drifting produced snow drifts up to two feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rushville law enforcement reported 10 inches of snowfall.  Road conditions were very poor with several inches of snow and slush.  In open areas, visibility was further reduced from blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 10 inches across the northern half of the county to 6 to 8 inches in the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous super cell thunderstorms developed across the Nebraska Sandhills and southwestern Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening hours on August 8th. These storms produced hail up to the size of baseballs in addition to wind gusts up to 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong sized hail and very heavy rain was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a four-hour period centered from 630-1030 pm CDT on this Sunday evening, a complex of severe thunderstorms tracked from northwest-to-southeast through primarily the southwestern two-thirds of South Central Nebraska, resulting in numerous reports of damaging winds ranging from 60 to around 80 MPH. Hail was not a significant issue, with nickel size stones being the largest reported. Although high winds also affected some counties farther west, the primary swath of damaging winds was roughly 20-40 miles wide, extending from the Valley/Sherman counties area in the northwest, southeastward through the Hall/Adams/Nuckolls counties areas. Along this main corridor, a sampling of the most wind significant reports in chronological order included: estimated 70 MPH winds in the Elyria and Litchfield areas with crop and tree damage, a measured 71 MPH gust near Wood River, a measured peak gust of 83 MPH at Hastings airport along with 10-20 minutes of persistent 60-80 MPH wind behind the initial surge, and up to 30 minutes of estimated 60-70 MPH gusts in Superior. Off to the west of this main zone of wind issues, there were a few reports of gusts around 60 MPH in the Lexington and Arapahoe areas. Rainfall-wise, the progressive nature of convection kept totals in most places no higher than 0.50-1.50 inches, with a few of the wetter exceptions per NeRAIN observes featuring 2.30 nine miles northwest of Ord and 2.13 six miles southeast of Davenport. ||In a basic sense, this event consisted of an initially semi-discrete supercell cluster that first developed in north central Nebraska during the mid-afternoon hours and then gradually grew upscale into a larger-scale, forward-propagating linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) as it crossed the local South Central Nebraska area. This intense storm complex first infiltrated the local area into Valley/Sherman/Dawson counties between 630-730 pm CDT, blew through the Grand Island/Hastings areas during the 815-930 pm CDT time frame and finally departed southeastward into northern Kansas between 10-11 pm CDT. Although showers/weak storms lingered over mainly eastern counties a bit longer, the vast majority of South Central Nebraska was void of precipitation by 1 am CDT on the 18th. ||By late-summer standards, this synoptic and mesoscale setup was fairly ripe for an organized severe weather event. In the mid-upper levels, a low-amplitude shortwave trough brushed into Nebraska from the northwest, along the southern periphery of a larger scale disturbance centered over southern Saskatchewan/Manitoba. As a result, mid-upper level flow increased enough to promote generally 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear over South Central Nebraska. In addition, instability was quite potent as the combination of afternoon surface temperatures in the upper-80s/near 90 F and dewpoints in the mid-60s/low-70s promoted mixed-layer CAPE values of 2000-4000 J/kg ahead of the incoming convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

During a four-hour period centered from 630-1030 pm CDT on this Sunday evening, a complex of severe thunderstorms tracked from northwest-to-southeast through primarily the southwestern two-thirds of South Central Nebraska, resulting in numerous reports of damaging winds ranging from 60 to around 80 MPH. Hail was not a significant issue, with nickel size stones being the largest reported. Although high winds also affected some counties farther west, the primary swath of damaging winds was roughly 20-40 miles wide, extending from the Valley/Sherman counties area in the northwest, southeastward through the Hall/Adams/Nuckolls counties areas. Along this main corridor, a sampling of the most wind significant reports in chronological order included: estimated 70 MPH winds in the Elyria and Litchfield areas with crop and tree damage, a measured 71 MPH gust near Wood River, a measured peak gust of 83 MPH at Hastings airport along with 10-20 minutes of persistent 60-80 MPH wind behind the initial surge, and up to 30 minutes of estimated 60-70 MPH gusts in Superior. Off to the west of this main zone of wind issues, there were a few reports of gusts around 60 MPH in the Lexington and Arapahoe areas. Rainfall-wise, the progressive nature of convection kept totals in most places no higher than 0.50-1.50 inches, with a few of the wetter exceptions per NeRAIN observes featuring 2.30 nine miles northwest of Ord and 2.13 six miles southeast of Davenport. ||In a basic sense, this event consisted of an initially semi-discrete supercell cluster that first developed in north central Nebraska during the mid-afternoon hours and then gradually grew upscale into a larger-scale, forward-propagating linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) as it crossed the local South Central Nebraska area. This intense storm complex first infiltrated the local area into Valley/Sherman/Dawson counties between 630-730 pm CDT, blew through the Grand Island/Hastings areas during the 815-930 pm CDT time frame and finally departed southeastward into northern Kansas between 10-11 pm CDT. Although showers/weak storms lingered over mainly eastern counties a bit longer, the vast majority of South Central Nebraska was void of precipitation by 1 am CDT on the 18th. ||By late-summer standards, this synoptic and mesoscale setup was fairly ripe for an organized severe weather event. In the mid-upper levels, a low-amplitude shortwave trough brushed into Nebraska from the northwest, along the southern periphery of a larger scale disturbance centered over southern Saskatchewan/Manitoba. As a result, mid-upper level flow increased enough to promote generally 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear over South Central Nebraska. In addition, instability was quite potent as the combination of afternoon surface temperatures in the upper-80s/near 90 F and dewpoints in the mid-60s/low-70s promoted mixed-layer CAPE values of 2000-4000 J/kg ahead of the incoming convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was measured at the Hastings NWS Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed across northeast Nebraska along a frontal boundary in a moderately unstable atmosphere. Deep layer shear was sufficient to produce isolated large hail over Knox and Cedar counties.

A couple thunderstorms produced low-end severe hail early on this Wednesday afternoon. These thunderstorms developed from scattered showers that were over southwestern Nebraska during the morning hours. As the showers moved into South Central Nebraska around midday, a couple of them intensified into thunderstorms. By 1:30 p.m. CDT, one of these two storms became severe, with multiple occurrences of nickel and quarter size hail across Sherman and Greeley counties. The other storm over Howard county produced a single occurrence of quarter size hail in the town of Elba.||The flow aloft was typical of summertime, with the polar jet zonal in character along the U.S.-Canada border and subtropical high pressure over the southern half of the country. A relatively potent shortwave trough was progressing through the Pacific Northwest. A low pressure system was associated with this trough over Montana. Nebraska was in the warm sector, between the warm front and a leeside trough/dryline that extended southward through western Nebraska. As the storms moved in, temperatures were near 80 and dewpoints were near 70. Mid-level lapse rates were very steep (8.5 degrees C/km). Combined with the boundary layer thermodynamic environment, this resulted in MLCAPE around 2000 J/kg. Despite the region being located south of the polar jet, deep layer shear was around 35 knots.

Waves of rain and embedded thunderstorms progressed through South Central Nebraska during this Tuesday, followed by additional thunderstorms in the late afternoon and early evening. The waves of rain and embedded storms began moving in during the predawn hours and lasted through the day. By late afternoon, the rain had largely ended. However, scattered thunderstorms then erupted east of Highway 281. The storms were most concentrated along Highway 30 from Grand Island northeast to Columbus. Some limited severe weather occurred, with several reports of one inch hail over Thayer county. There was a single reported occurrence of one inch hail associated with the storms along Highway 30, near the town of Benedict. The main issue was excessive rainfall in this corridor. From 5:30 to 7:30 p.m. CDT storms repeatedly formed and trained. Thereafter, the line of storms sagged southeastward across Hamilton, Polk and York counties. Many observers measured three to nearly four inches of rain over Polk and York counties, much of which fell within a two to three hour period.||The flow aloft was zonal but exhibited a broad trough across the northern United States. An embedded shortwave trough was advancing through the Pacific Northwest. A suppressed subtropical high was over Texas. A strong surface cold front extended from eastern Canada across the Northern Plains and into the Northern Rockies. This front very slowly sagged southward into south central Nebraska during the day and exited to the south and east during the evening. Low-level winds of 30-40 knots south of the front (850 mb) advected very rich dewpoints, as high as +17C, northward into the front during the day. Precipitable water levels were around 2 inches and this was above the 99th percentile for the date for all the PW observations in the Omaha area since 1948. Because of the extensive rain and clouds, temperatures were suppressed and afternoon instability was modest, ranging from 500 J/kg of MLCAPE near Highway 30 to 1250 J/kg near the Kansas-Nebraska border. Deep layer shear ranged from 40-50 knots.

Waves of rain and embedded thunderstorms progressed through South Central Nebraska during this Tuesday, followed by additional thunderstorms in the late afternoon and early evening. The waves of rain and embedded storms began moving in during the predawn hours and lasted through the day. By late afternoon, the rain had largely ended. However, scattered thunderstorms then erupted east of Highway 281. The storms were most concentrated along Highway 30 from Grand Island northeast to Columbus. Some limited severe weather occurred, with several reports of one inch hail over Thayer county. There was a single reported occurrence of one inch hail associated with the storms along Highway 30, near the town of Benedict. The main issue was excessive rainfall in this corridor. From 5:30 to 7:30 p.m. CDT storms repeatedly formed and trained. Thereafter, the line of storms sagged southeastward across Hamilton, Polk and York counties. Many observers measured three to nearly four inches of rain over Polk and York counties, much of which fell within a two to three hour period.||The flow aloft was zonal but exhibited a broad trough across the northern United States. An embedded shortwave trough was advancing through the Pacific Northwest. A suppressed subtropical high was over Texas. A strong surface cold front extended from eastern Canada across the Northern Plains and into the Northern Rockies. This front very slowly sagged southward into south central Nebraska during the day and exited to the south and east during the evening. Low-level winds of 30-40 knots south of the front (850 mb) advected very rich dewpoints, as high as +17C, northward into the front during the day. Precipitable water levels were around 2 inches and this was above the 99th percentile for the date for all the PW observations in the Omaha area since 1948. Because of the extensive rain and clouds, temperatures were suppressed and afternoon instability was modest, ranging from 500 J/kg of MLCAPE near Highway 30 to 1250 J/kg near the Kansas-Nebraska border. Deep layer shear ranged from 40-50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall of 3.10 inches was reported, most falling between 6-9 p.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Wednesday evening into early Thursday morning featured South Central Nebraska's last bout of strong to severe thunderstorms in 2014. Although radar data suggested that isolated severe convection lingered into the early morning hours of the 2nd, all ground-truth severe weather reports occurred on the 1st between 730-1130 pm CDT, in areas west of Highway 14 and south of Highway 92. The highlight of the evening was an elevated supercell that tracked east generally down Highway 6 through Adams and Clay counties between 8-10 pm CDT, yielding numerous reports of hail up to half dollar size in places including Hastings. Along with the hail, this storm produced strong to marginally severe wind including measured gusts of 55 MPH at Hastings airport and 66 MPH at a mesonet site five miles southwest of Harvard. Not surprisingly, this combination of hail and wind damaged siding on area homes. Outside the path of this primary severe storm, there were several other quarter size hail reports during the evening, including near Naponee and Franklin in Franklin County and at Amherst and near Miller in Buffalo County. Due to the fairly progressive nature of most convection, the majority of South Central Nebraska received no more than 0.50-1.00 inch of rain, although small pockets topped out closer to two inches, including 1.88 two miles west of Kenesaw (NeRAIN). ||Taking a closer look at convective mode and timing, the first isolated strong to severe storms flared up between 630-800 pm CDT within various central counties including Franklin, Kearney, Adams and Buffalo. As this initial isolated activity drifted east or northeast during the ensuing few hours, by far the most organized storm was the one over Adams/Clay counties, which assumed elevated supercell characteristics. A bit later in the evening mainly between 10 pm-12 am CDT, another isolated strong to severe storm drifted across northeastern Dawson and northern Buffalo counties. However, the main feature of the later evening and post-midnight hours was a north-to-south oriented squall line that first entered far western portions of South Central Nebraska between 11 pm-12 am CDT. Initially, it appeared this convective line might become legitimately severe, but in fact it yielded only a few reports of sub-severe hail and wind as it limped into the Highway 281 corridor counties by 2 am CDT. Just as it appeared any severe threat might be over with for the night, the southern end of the line morphed into a semi-discrete, radar-indicated severe storm that tracked across mainly southern portions of Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer counties between 2-4 am CDT before exiting the local area. However, this storm yielded no verifying ground-truth reports, and in its wake, only scattered showers and a few very weak thunderstorms lingered over South Central Nebraska through the remainder of the night.  ||On the synoptic scale aloft, this early-autumn severe event focused just downstream from a sharpening mid-upper trough axis aligned from the far western Dakotas to the Four Corners region at midnight. Some of the initial early-evening convection was likely aided by subtle forcing from a weak lead wave out ahead of this parent trough. At the surface during the evening, forcing for convection was also rather nebulous, although a weak trough axis was draped through the general area. Toward midnight and through the early morning hours of the 2nd, a more organized cold front pushed through South Central Nebraska and helped promote the increasing areal coverage of convection. Instability-wise, this was not an overly-favorable setup for severe weather as surface dewpoints only averaged in the mid-50s F and most-unstable CAPE only peaked around 1000 J/kg. However, 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear was fairly strong at generally 40-50 knots, helping to enhance storm organization.

A strong thunderstorm produced nickel size hail on this Tuesday evening. In the middle to late afternoon hours, a broken arc of thunderstorms developed over the Sandhills. Most thunderstorms did not survive, but those that did formed a short line segment across Custer County during the early evening hours. That line diminished in size as it headed southeastward into south central Nebraska. However, other scattered storms developed in its wake, possibly along the trailing composite outflow generated from all of the convection. High pressure was over the eastern United States with a modified Continental Polar air mass in place. Return flow had advected a narrow plume of moisture northward through the Plains, immediately east of a leeside trough. MUCAPE was near 1000 J/kg and deep layer shear was around 35 knots.

This Wednesday evening into early Thursday morning featured South Central Nebraska's last bout of strong to severe thunderstorms in 2014. Although radar data suggested that isolated severe convection lingered into the early morning hours of the 2nd, all ground-truth severe weather reports occurred on the 1st between 730-1130 pm CDT, in areas west of Highway 14 and south of Highway 92. The highlight of the evening was an elevated supercell that tracked east generally down Highway 6 through Adams and Clay counties between 8-10 pm CDT, yielding numerous reports of hail up to half dollar size in places including Hastings. Along with the hail, this storm produced strong to marginally severe wind including measured gusts of 55 MPH at Hastings airport and 66 MPH at a mesonet site five miles southwest of Harvard. Not surprisingly, this combination of hail and wind damaged siding on area homes. Outside the path of this primary severe storm, there were several other quarter size hail reports during the evening, including near Naponee and Franklin in Franklin County and at Amherst and near Miller in Buffalo County. Due to the fairly progressive nature of most convection, the majority of South Central Nebraska received no more than 0.50-1.00 inch of rain, although small pockets topped out closer to two inches, including 1.88 two miles west of Kenesaw (NeRAIN). ||Taking a closer look at convective mode and timing, the first isolated strong to severe storms flared up between 630-800 pm CDT within various central counties including Franklin, Kearney, Adams and Buffalo. As this initial isolated activity drifted east or northeast during the ensuing few hours, by far the most organized storm was the one over Adams/Clay counties, which assumed elevated supercell characteristics. A bit later in the evening mainly between 10 pm-12 am CDT, another isolated strong to severe storm drifted across northeastern Dawson and northern Buffalo counties. However, the main feature of the later evening and post-midnight hours was a north-to-south oriented squall line that first entered far western portions of South Central Nebraska between 11 pm-12 am CDT. Initially, it appeared this convective line might become legitimately severe, but in fact it yielded only a few reports of sub-severe hail and wind as it limped into the Highway 281 corridor counties by 2 am CDT. Just as it appeared any severe threat might be over with for the night, the southern end of the line morphed into a semi-discrete, radar-indicated severe storm that tracked across mainly southern portions of Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer counties between 2-4 am CDT before exiting the local area. However, this storm yielded no verifying ground-truth reports, and in its wake, only scattered showers and a few very weak thunderstorms lingered over South Central Nebraska through the remainder of the night.  ||On the synoptic scale aloft, this early-autumn severe event focused just downstream from a sharpening mid-upper trough axis aligned from the far western Dakotas to the Four Corners region at midnight. Some of the initial early-evening convection was likely aided by subtle forcing from a weak lead wave out ahead of this parent trough. At the surface during the evening, forcing for convection was also rather nebulous, although a weak trough axis was draped through the general area. Toward midnight and through the early morning hours of the 2nd, a more organized cold front pushed through South Central Nebraska and helped promote the increasing areal coverage of convection. Instability-wise, this was not an overly-favorable setup for severe weather as surface dewpoints only averaged in the mid-50s F and most-unstable CAPE only peaked around 1000 J/kg. However, 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear was fairly strong at generally 40-50 knots, helping to enhance storm organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported at 830 pm CDT about 2 miles southwest of Hastings, which was reported by a trained spotter. Quarter size hail was also reported at 833 pm CDT about 4 miles southwest of Hastings, which was reported by the public.

Outside the path of this primary severe storm, there were several other quarter size hail reports during the evening, including near Naponee and Franklin in Franklin County and at Amherst and near Miller in Buffalo County. Due to the fairly progressive nature of most convection, the majority of South Central Nebraska received no more than 0.50-1.00 inch of rain, although small pockets topped out closer to two inches, including 1.88 two miles west of Kenesaw (NeRAIN). ||Taking a closer look at convective mode and timing, the first isolated strong to severe storms flared up between 630-800 pm CDT within various central counties including Franklin, Kearney, Adams and Buffalo. As this initial isolated activity drifted east or northeast during the ensuing few hours, by far the most organized storm was the one over Adams/Clay counties, which assumed elevated supercell characteristics. A bit later in the evening mainly between 10 pm-12 am CDT, another isolated strong to severe storm drifted across northeastern Dawson and northern Buffalo counties. However, the main feature of the later evening and post-midnight hours was a north-to-south oriented squall line that first entered far western portions of South Central Nebraska between 11 pm-12 am CDT. Initially, it appeared this convective line might become legitimately severe, but in fact it yielded only a few reports of sub-severe hail and wind as it limped into the Highway 281 corridor counties by 2 am CDT. Just as it appeared any severe threat might be over with for the night, the southern end of the line morphed into a semi-discrete, radar-indicated severe storm that tracked across mainly southern portions of Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer counties between 2-4 am CDT before exiting the local area. However, this storm yielded no verifying ground-truth reports, and in its wake, only scattered showers and a few very weak thunderstorms lingered over South Central Nebraska through the remainder of the night.  ||On the synoptic scale aloft, this early-autumn severe event focused just downstream from a sharpening mid-upper trough axis aligned from the far western Dakotas to the Four Corners region at midnight. Some of the initial early-evening convection was likely aided by subtle forcing from a weak lead wave out ahead of this parent trough. At the surface during the evening, forcing for convection was also rather nebulous, although a weak trough axis was draped through the general area. Toward midnight and through the early morning hours of the 2nd, a more organized cold front pushed through South Central Nebraska and helped promote the increasing areal coverage of convection. Instability-wise, this was not an overly-favorable setup for severe weather as surface dewpoints only averaged in the mid-50s F and most-unstable CAPE only peaked around 1000 J/kg. However, 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear was fairly strong at generally 40-50 knots, helping to enhance storm organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was received from Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of rain and embedded thunderstorms progressed through South Central Nebraska during this Tuesday, followed by additional thunderstorms in the late afternoon and early evening. The waves of rain and embedded storms began moving in during the predawn hours and lasted through the day. By late afternoon, the rain had largely ended. However, scattered thunderstorms then erupted east of Highway 281. The storms were most concentrated along Highway 30 from Grand Island northeast to Columbus. Some limited severe weather occurred, with several reports of one inch hail over Thayer county. There was a single reported occurrence of one inch hail associated with the storms along Highway 30, near the town of Benedict. The main issue was excessive rainfall in this corridor. From 5:30 to 7:30 p.m. CDT storms repeatedly formed and trained. Thereafter, the line of storms sagged southeastward across Hamilton, Polk and York counties. Many observers measured three to nearly four inches of rain over Polk and York counties, much of which fell within a two to three hour period.||The flow aloft was zonal but exhibited a broad trough across the northern United States. An embedded shortwave trough was advancing through the Pacific Northwest. A suppressed subtropical high was over Texas. A strong surface cold front extended from eastern Canada across the Northern Plains and into the Northern Rockies. This front very slowly sagged southward into south central Nebraska during the day and exited to the south and east during the evening. Low-level winds of 30-40 knots south of the front (850 mb) advected very rich dewpoints, as high as +17C, northward into the front during the day. Precipitable water levels were around 2 inches and this was above the 99th percentile for the date for all the PW observations in the Omaha area since 1948. Because of the extensive rain and clouds, temperatures were suppressed and afternoon instability was modest, ranging from 500 J/kg of MLCAPE near Highway 30 to 1250 J/kg near the Kansas-Nebraska border. Deep layer shear ranged from 40-50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH and were accompanied by quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from repeated thunderstorms in late September caused a number of rivers in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa to go into flood. Much of the flooding was minor, but flooding along the Salt Creek and associated tributaries was more significant and in some locations exceeded 100 and 200 year return intervals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of September caused the Missouri River to go into flood. The river rose above flood stage in the afternoon on the 1st, crested and fell below flood stage that evening. The river flooding was minor and mainly confined to low-lying areas along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally wet period with repeated thunderstorms from August into early September led to a number of area rivers having above normal river levels. A slow moving complex of thunderstorms near the 10th of September set rivers in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa above flood stage. This included the Missouri and the Big Blue Rivers in Nebraska, and the East Nishnabotna, main stem of the Nishnabotna, and Nodaway Rivers in southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missouri River reached flood stage near Brownville on the 10th and created in the early morning hours of the 11th at 34.95 feet. The river fell below flood stage on the morning of the 12th. The flooding was confined to low areas along the river.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from late August continued into early September along the Big Blue River. The river crested in late August, but continued in minor flooding until the early morning on the 4th. The flooding was confined to low-lying areas along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface warm front extending west to east across northern Kansas the afternoon of September 9th slowly moved north into southeastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Surface based storms developed along and just north of this front, and quickly became supercellular. One HP supercell developed over Otoe county produced an EF1 tornado. Elevated storms formed during the afternoon well north of the surface boundary on the leading edge of an 850 mb front. This was ahead of a strong shortwave moving into the central plains. A moisture rich environment led to very heavy rain with several reports of flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed from a slow moving supercell across rural Otoe County. The tornado initially developed near County Road 50, and between County Road M and L. Initial damage was mainly to crops and snapped tree limbs. The slow-moving tornado traveled generally east southeast exhibiting a slightly erratic path through rural Otoe County. The tornado impacted a couple of farmsteads along  County Road 56, and also along County Road M. At these locations outbuildings were damaged or destroyed, as well as considerable tree damage. The tornado cross U.S. Highway 75 at the intersection of Nebraska Highway 128 where tree and power line damage occurred. The tornado continued east along Highway 128 continuing to do tree  and crop damage for 1 mile before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple thunderstorms produced low-end severe hail early on this Wednesday afternoon. These thunderstorms developed from scattered showers that were over southwestern Nebraska during the morning hours. As the showers moved into South Central Nebraska around midday, a couple of them intensified into thunderstorms. By 1:30 p.m. CDT, one of these two storms became severe, with multiple occurrences of nickel and quarter size hail across Sherman and Greeley counties. The other storm over Howard county produced a single occurrence of quarter size hail in the town of Elba.||The flow aloft was typical of summertime, with the polar jet zonal in character along the U.S.-Canada border and subtropical high pressure over the southern half of the country. A relatively potent shortwave trough was progressing through the Pacific Northwest. A low pressure system was associated with this trough over Montana. Nebraska was in the warm sector, between the warm front and a leeside trough/dryline that extended southward through western Nebraska. As the storms moved in, temperatures were near 80 and dewpoints were near 70. Mid-level lapse rates were very steep (8.5 degrees C/km). Combined with the boundary layer thermodynamic environment, this resulted in MLCAPE around 2000 J/kg. Despite the region being located south of the polar jet, deep layer shear was around 35 knots.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey of the track of the slow-moving Otoe County supercell revealed a swath of damage winds associated with the forward flank to the north of the tornado. This damage started just south of Nebraska Highway 2 about 3 miles east of Dunbar and continued east southeast across the south side of Nebraska and to the Missouri River. The damage consisted of flattened corn and broken or snapped tree limbs.

Some reports of near 5.00 inches in the northern part of the county were reported. High water trapped vehicles in some Omaha areas, including under the bridge near Westroads Mall at West Dodge Road and Regency Parkway. When Omaha firefighters arrived, they spotted a minivan in nearly 3 feet of water. Firefighters helped a mother and three children from their vehicle. Other vehicles had to deal with flooding at 96th and Q Streets, 84th and Cass Streets and 90th and Burt Streets. Saddle Creek Road was flooded under the Dodge Street bridge. Runoff that overwhelmed the city's storm sewers caused manhole covers to pop off in several locations around the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed across northeast Nebraska along a frontal boundary in a moderately unstable atmosphere. Deep layer shear was sufficient to produce isolated large hail over Knox and Cedar counties.

Starting during the evening hours of Saturday the 22nd, peaking in intensity overnight and gradually moving out by late morning on the 23rd, a notably-narrow band of heavy snow in the 6-10 inch range targeted mainly the southwestern-most counties of South Central Nebraska. Meanwhile, the majority of the 24-county area outside this heavy banding observed little to no accumulation. The primary snow band of 6+ inches generally extended no more than 10 miles either side of a line stretched from Cambridge to Harlan County Lake, meaning that Furnas, Harlan and southern Gosper counties bore the brunt of the action. The highest unofficial snow total of 10 originated from Stamford, while a few of the highest NWS cooperative observer reports included 8.5 at Cambridge, 8 at Edison and 7 in Beaver City. Only a short distance north-northeast of the primary band, the Holdrege and Minden areas observed no more than 1-2. Fortunately, winds were not a major issue during this event, with sustained northeasterly speeds of generally 10-15 MPH keeping blowing and drifting to a minimum. ||Timing-wise, this quasi-stationary heavy snow band first started organizing over the area between 7-9 PM, peaked in intensity during the overnight hours, and gradually weakened and slid off to the east during the post-sunrise hours on the morning of the 23rd. Not surprisingly, the meteorological culprit for this narrow, banded snow event was a concentrated zone of mid level frontogenesis evident at the 700 millibar level, driven by lift from passing upper level jet streaks. On the larger scale aloft, a broad, low-amplitude trough encompassed most of the nation. At the surface during this snow event, South Central Nebraska became increasingly under the influence of high pressure building southward out of southern Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 8.5 inches fell in the community of Cambridge, 8.0 inches in Edison, 7.0 inches in Beaver City, and 6.0 inches in Wilsonville. A lesser amount of 4.5 inches was reported 6 miles north-northwest of Oxford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In March significant snow fell in narrow bands that led to below normal precipitations for portions of the northern sandhills, southwest and up to 2 inches below normal for areas east of a line from Spencer to Merna. The areas of below normal precipitation led to continued Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions in the west, yet also caused an expansion of drought conditions into portions of north central Nebraska to include portions of Blaine, Loup, Garfield, and Custer counties as Moderate (D1) conditions worsened into Severe (D2) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered. Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought conditions continued into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One long-tracked supercell thunderstorm, and several other shorter-lived storms spawned tornadoes across eastern Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 11th.  The storms developed near the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in south central Nebraska then tracked northeast along the warm front toward the Lincoln and Omaha areas.   Conditions in the area were very unstable with temperatures in the 80s to around 90 south of the warm front with dew point temperatures in the 60s.  In total 12 tornadoes were recorded in the Omaha/Valley warning area with several others in the Hastings forecast area. The storms also produced very heavy rainfall which caused some flooding, mostly north through west and southwest of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed in open country southeast of Daykin and traveled northeast into Saline county with a total track of 15.5 miles (5.8 miles in Jefferson county).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was advancing east across central Nebraska around dawn on this Tuesday. Low pressure was along the front over Kansas and was moving east. The front continued eastward through the daytime hours and was at the Iowa border by sunset. The Kansas low moved to the Kansas City area. A broad upper level trough was over the United States, with a potent embedded shortwave trough advancing through the Intermountain West. At the base of this trough, a 120 knot upper level jet streak was ejecting into the Southern Plains. This put the Central Plains in the favorable quadrant for strong and deep upward motion. This system did not have much moisture to work with, so the amount of snow falling around the mid-level portion of the system over western Nebraska was paltry during the previous night and early morning hours. By mid-morning, however, a snow band began organizing from McCook to North Platte to Ainsworth. This snow band increased in size and intensity and advanced into south central Nebraska, appearing to curl around the back side of the mid-level circulation.||Generally, 2 to 6 inches of snow fell along and west of Highway 183. The highest amounts fell along and west of Highway 283. From 11 a.m. to 2 p.m., heavy snow fell over Furnas and western Dawson counties, along the southern edge of the band. The high snowfall rates and winds resulted in near blizzard conditions. Visibilities were reported near zero over western Dawson County. North winds with sustained speeds around 30 mph were recorded by the automated sites located at the Lexington and Holdrege airports, with peak gusts between 40 and 45 mph. The highest snowfall totals were in and around Gothenburg. A precipitation observer for the state of Nebraska reported over 12 inches, and an article from the Gothenburg Times mentioned 13 inches. The intensity of the snowband rapidly diminished after 2 p.m., as it advanced further across south central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest storm total snowfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One long-tracked supercell thunderstorm, and several other shorter-lived storms spawned tornadoes across eastern Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 11th.  The storms developed near the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in south central Nebraska then tracked northeast along the warm front toward the Lincoln and Omaha areas.   Conditions in the area were very unstable with temperatures in the 80s to around 90 south of the warm front with dew point temperatures in the 60s.  In total 12 tornadoes were recorded in the Omaha/Valley warning area with several others in the Hastings forecast area. The storms also produced very heavy rainfall which caused some flooding, mostly north through west and southwest of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across parts of north Lincoln, including across 44th Street near Cornhusker Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From southwest Nebraska into eastern portions of north central Nebraska, much below normal precipitation ranging from a inch to locally more than two inches occurred for the month of April. The area of Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions remained the same across much of southwest Nebraska into the central Sandhills to end the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered. Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought conditions continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In March significant snow fell in narrow bands that led to below normal precipitations for portions of the northern sandhills, southwest and up to 2 inches below normal for areas east of a line from Spencer to Merna. The areas of below normal precipitation led to continued Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions in the west, yet also caused an expansion of drought conditions into portions of north central Nebraska to include portions of Blaine, Loup, Garfield, and Custer counties as Moderate (D1) conditions worsened into Severe (D2) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered as Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to Severe (D2) drought conditions. The drought continued into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, March 2014 marked the 22nd-consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued some portion of this 24-county South Central Nebraska area, continuing one of the most notable droughts in decades. For most of the area, March was at least the fifth-consecutive month of below normal precipitation, but compared to 30-year normals it was hands-down the driest month of all recent ones. According to reports from nearly 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also taking into account radar-derived precipitation estimates, all of South Central Nebraska measured below normal March precipitation (based on long-term March normals of roughly 1.85). However, more significantly, nearly 90% of the area received less than one-fourth of normal March precipitation. Given these dubious March precipitation trends, the weekly U.S. Drought Monitor reflected a one-category degradation in drought classification across much of the 24-county area. At month's end, roughly 75% of South Central Nebraska was either in category D1 Moderate Drought or D2 Severe Drought. The D1 Moderate Drought area included several counties along and west of Highway 281, along with Nance and Merrick counties and portions of Nuckolls, Thayer and Polk counties farther east. Meanwhile, worse D2 Severe Drought continued to plague all or parts of western counties such as Furnas, Gosper, Dawson, Harlan and Phelps, and now included most of Valley County as well. Farther east, all of Hamilton, York, Clay and Fillmore counties along with at least small portions of several bordering counties continued to fare a bit better with D0 Abnormally Dry classification (considered to be one category better than ���true��� drought). ||Not surprisingly, many locations within the area endured one of the Top-10 driest months of March on record, including Grand Island airport with its 9th-driest out of 117 years with only 0.19, and Hastings airport which also had its 9th-driest out of 115 years with merely 0.15. Even more impressively, Red Cloud tied with a handful of previous years for the outright-driest March on record out of 118 years with only a paltry trace. Although still plenty dry, there was one localized exception within the area that received around one-half to as much as two-thirds of normal March precipitation: mainly portions of Dawson, Gosper and Furnas counties. In those counties, most precipitation fell on the 18th and/or 22nd in the form of several inches of snow. As for monthly extremes, a few of the lowest March totals per NWS cooperative observers included only a trace at Red Cloud and Bruning, 0.03 at Hebron and 0.05 at Hubbell. On the comparably ���wetter��� side of the spectrum were western stations including Gothenburg (1.30���), Cambridge (0.81���) and eight miles south of Elwood (0.73). According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of March, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies degraded slightly since late-February, as the short to very short categories increased from 57% to 62%. Subsoil moisture supplies followed a similar trend, degrading from 55% to 60% short to very short.

Strong arctic high pressure was over the Canadian Prairies on this Saturday, and its southern expanse encompassed most of the central United States. After a couple mild days in the 50s and 60s, temperatures plummeted. The surface weather maps changed little through the course of the day, and in the upper levels, flow was zonal over much of the country. An embedded low amplitude shortwave trough advanced from the Intermountain West onto the Central Plains by evening. A 110 knot upper level jet streak preceded this trough and by evening had shifted eastward enough that its entrance region became located over the Central Plains, creating a favorable environment for deep upward motion.||Around midday, a narrow (roughly one county wide) band of light snow developed along and just south of highway 6. The band initially developed over Gosper, Phelps, and Kearney counties, and then sagged into Furnas, Harlan, and Franklin counties through the mid- and late-afternoon hours. By 7 pm, the band had progressed across the state border into Kansas. Three to 5 inches of snow fell across portions of Furnas and Gosper counties, with lesser amounts in neighboring counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall averaged 3 to 5 inches across the county.  A few reports from the area included 5 inches in Wilsonville and 5 miles south of Hendley, 4.2 inches in Cambridge, 3.5 inches in Beaver City and 3 inches in Edison.
