EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted as Severe (D2) drought conditions continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From southwest Nebraska into eastern portions of north central Nebraska, much below normal precipitation ranging from a inch to locally more than two inches occurred for the month of April. The area of Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions remained the same across much of southwest Nebraska into the central Sandhills to end the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 persisted as Severe (D2) drought conditions continued across the southern half of the county into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the evening hours of Saturday the 22nd, peaking in intensity overnight and gradually moving out by late morning on the 23rd, a notably-narrow band of heavy snow in the 6-10 inch range targeted mainly the southwestern-most counties of South Central Nebraska. Meanwhile, the majority of the 24-county area outside this heavy banding observed little to no accumulation. The primary snow band of 6+ inches generally extended no more than 10 miles either side of a line stretched from Cambridge to Harlan County Lake, meaning that Furnas, Harlan and southern Gosper counties bore the brunt of the action. The highest unofficial snow total of 10 originated from Stamford, while a few of the highest NWS cooperative observer reports included 8.5 at Cambridge, 8 at Edison and 7 in Beaver City. Only a short distance north-northeast of the primary band, the Holdrege and Minden areas observed no more than 1-2. Fortunately, winds were not a major issue during this event, with sustained northeasterly speeds of generally 10-15 MPH keeping blowing and drifting to a minimum. ||Timing-wise, this quasi-stationary heavy snow band first started organizing over the area between 7-9 PM, peaked in intensity during the overnight hours, and gradually weakened and slid off to the east during the post-sunrise hours on the morning of the 23rd. Not surprisingly, the meteorological culprit for this narrow, banded snow event was a concentrated zone of mid level frontogenesis evident at the 700 millibar level, driven by lift from passing upper level jet streaks. On the larger scale aloft, a broad, low-amplitude trough encompassed most of the nation. At the surface during this snow event, South Central Nebraska became increasingly under the influence of high pressure building southward out of southern Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 10.0 inches fell in the community of Stamford. A sharply lesser amount of 5.0 inches was reported from the cooperative observer at Harlan County Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In March significant snow fell in narrow bands that led to below normal precipitations for portions of the northern sandhills, southwest and up to 2 inches below normal for areas east of a line from Spencer to Merna. The areas of below normal precipitation led to continued Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions in the west, yet also caused an expansion of drought conditions into portions of north central Nebraska to include portions of Blaine, Loup, Garfield, and Custer counties as Moderate (D1) conditions worsened into Severe (D2) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 intensified in March over Garfield County. The Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to include Severe (D2) drought conditions across southern Garfield County and continued into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to portions of central and north central Nebraska  on March 18, 2014. As the storm system moved east heavy snow bands developed that led to snowfall amounts from 6 to 9 inches in portions of Rock, Blaine, Loup, and Custer counties. The banded snowfall created locally higher snow amounts to 12 inches in western Custer County to include the communities of Arnold and Callaway. Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusty, created significant blowing and drifting snow that led to near whiteouts and drifting snow during the afternoon hours. The combination of blowing and drifting resulted in the closure of Highway 92 from Arnold east to Merna. One fatality occurred indirectly from a rear end collision, where a 57 year old male died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the western half of Custer County during the late morning and afternoon hours on March 18th, 2014. A Cooperative Observer in Arnold reported 12 inches of snowfall, while a Cooperative Observer 8 miles west of Callaway reported 11 inches of snowfall. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches were reported across the western half of Custer County. Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusty caused poor visibility and considerable blowing and drifting snow. The poor weather conditions were indirectly responsible for one fatality and one injury in a two vehicle collision on Highway 47 in southwest Custer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One long-tracked supercell thunderstorm, and several other shorter-lived storms spawned tornadoes across eastern Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 11th.  The storms developed near the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in south central Nebraska then tracked northeast along the warm front toward the Lincoln and Omaha areas.   Conditions in the area were very unstable with temperatures in the 80s to around 90 south of the warm front with dew point temperatures in the 60s.  In total 12 tornadoes were recorded in the Omaha/Valley warning area with several others in the Hastings forecast area. The storms also produced very heavy rainfall which caused some flooding, mostly north through west and southwest of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was the second of two tornadoes that hit near the Omaha area this evening.  The track of this tornado was around 5 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was advancing east across central Nebraska around dawn on this Tuesday. Low pressure was along the front over Kansas and was moving east. The front continued eastward through the daytime hours and was at the Iowa border by sunset. The Kansas low moved to the Kansas City area. A broad upper level trough was over the United States, with a potent embedded shortwave trough advancing through the Intermountain West. At the base of this trough, a 120 knot upper level jet streak was ejecting into the Southern Plains. This put the Central Plains in the favorable quadrant for strong and deep upward motion. This system did not have much moisture to work with, so the amount of snow falling around the mid-level portion of the system over western Nebraska was paltry during the previous night and early morning hours. By mid-morning, however, a snow band began organizing from McCook to North Platte to Ainsworth. This snow band increased in size and intensity and advanced into south central Nebraska, appearing to curl around the back side of the mid-level circulation.||Generally, 2 to 6 inches of snow fell along and west of Highway 183. The highest amounts fell along and west of Highway 283. From 11 a.m. to 2 p.m., heavy snow fell over Furnas and western Dawson counties, along the southern edge of the band. The high snowfall rates and winds resulted in near blizzard conditions. Visibilities were reported near zero over western Dawson County. North winds with sustained speeds around 30 mph were recorded by the automated sites located at the Lexington and Holdrege airports, with peak gusts between 40 and 45 mph. The highest snowfall totals were in and around Gothenburg. A precipitation observer for the state of Nebraska reported over 12 inches, and an article from the Gothenburg Times mentioned 13 inches. The intensity of the snowband rapidly diminished after 2 p.m., as it advanced further across south central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest storm total snowfall ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the county. NWS Cooperative Observer reports included 6 inches in Cambridge and 4 inches in Wilsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved into the central Plains during the day on Sunday April 27th. This system brought more widespread severe weather to areas of the southern Plains into the southeast United States, but for eastern Nebraska morning showers and thunderstorms, some of which were strong, severely limited the degree of instability that was available during the afternoon when a deep area of low pressure moved into central Nebraska. As a result only isolated thunderstorms were able to develop, and mainly confined to areas of central into northeast Nebraska where isolated severe weather in the form of large hail occurred. With the storm system still in the area on Monday the 28th, isolated strong storms again developed in the afternoon producing small hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on Tuesday the 4th, accumulating snow overspread all of South Central Nebraska from south to north, peaking in intensity during the afternoon and early evening hours before gradually diminishing overnight and ending before sunrise on the 5th. While much of the northwest half of the 24-county area reported storm total accumulations of generally 2 inches or less, several counties primarily southeast of a line from Franklin-Hastings-Osceola received a broad coverage of 3-4. The extreme southeast corner of the local area, namely Thayer County, saw isolated totals in excess of 6, including 8 reported by the NWS cooperative observer in Hubbell. A few of the other highest cooperative observer snow totals featured 4.8 four miles east of Superior, 4.5 at Bruning and 4 at Red Cloud. In the Tri-Cities area, totals ranged from only 1.2 at Kearney to 3.2 at Hastings. ||Thanks to very cold temperatures that only averaged between 5-15 degrees during most of the event, snow was light and fluffy in character. Winds were also an issue, as sustained northerly speeds of generally 20-25 MPH and gusts to around 35 MPH promoted drifting snow and frequent visibility reductions to around one-half mile under the heaviest snow bands. The combination of winds and frigid temperatures dropped wind chill values into the -10 to -20 range from the late afternoon into the overnight hours. Snow first started pushing out of Kansas into southern portions of the area mainly between 7-9 AM, but the majority of impactful, accumulating snow within South Central Nebraska fell during the 11 AM-6 PM time frame, with only minor accumulations lingering into the night. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by a large, amplified trough that tracked northeast through the Central and Southern Plains. Given that the closed 700 millibar low pressure center tracked across the southern half of Kansas into Missouri, the local area was spared the highest snow totals of at least 9-12 that targeted parts of central and northeast Kansas. At the surface, a pronounced Arctic cold front plunged southward through South Central Nebraska during the morning hours, ushering in the frigid temperatures and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.0 inches fell in the communities of Beaver City and Wilsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very windy conditions occurred over south central Nebraska on this Wednesday, especially east of Highway 183. High pressure was sprawled over the eastern United States, with a cold front advancing through the Intermountain West. Along this front, low pressure ejected from Wyoming into South Dakota. The result was a tight pressure gradient over the Central Plains. Unlike most high wind events, which occur with north winds, the high winds on this day were from the south. Numerous wind gusts over 50 mph were measured. Three locations, including Wood River, Shelby, and Glenvil measured peak gusts of 58 mph during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet site located 1 mile east-northeast of Shelby.

The storms developed near the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in south central Nebraska then tracked northeast along the warm front toward the Lincoln and Omaha areas.   Conditions in the area were very unstable with temperatures in the 80s to around 90 south of the warm front with dew point temperatures in the 60s.  In total 12 tornadoes were recorded in the Omaha/Valley warning area with several others in the Hastings forecast area. The storms also produced very heavy rainfall which caused some flooding, mostly north through west and southwest of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were estimated at between 85 and 90 mph near the I80 interchange north of Milford.  The winds were possibly outflow from the rear flank downdraft with the supercell thunderstorm that produced several tornadoes in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Normal to above normal precipitation fell across the central and western Sandhills as well as southwestern Nebraska during the month of February.  Total precipitation ranged from a half an inch in southwestern Nebraska, to around an inch in the central Sandhills.  In light of the normal to above normal precipitation, several counties remained in severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions over the central and western Sandhills...as well as southwestern Nebraska.  The area of severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought remained unchanged during the month of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 continued through February 2014. Severe (D2) drought continued into March across south central and southwestern Cherry County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system produced high winds across portions of the west on April 28, 2014. The winds quickly increased during the morning hours and persisted through the afternoon as northwest winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts to near 60 mph were observed. The strong winds were reported to have destroyed a grain bin in Hershey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby Ogallala AWOS recorded sustained winds of 40 mph with occasional gusts to 50 mph between 12 pm MDT and 3 pm MDT. Northwest winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 50 mph were common across Arthur County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on Tuesday the 4th, accumulating snow overspread all of South Central Nebraska from south to north, peaking in intensity during the afternoon and early evening hours before gradually diminishing overnight and ending before sunrise on the 5th. While much of the northwest half of the 24-county area reported storm total accumulations of generally 2 inches or less, several counties primarily southeast of a line from Franklin-Hastings-Osceola received a broad coverage of 3-4. The extreme southeast corner of the local area, namely Thayer County, saw isolated totals in excess of 6, including 8 reported by the NWS cooperative observer in Hubbell. A few of the other highest cooperative observer snow totals featured 4.8 four miles east of Superior, 4.5 at Bruning and 4 at Red Cloud. In the Tri-Cities area, totals ranged from only 1.2 at Kearney to 3.2 at Hastings. ||Thanks to very cold temperatures that only averaged between 5-15 degrees during most of the event, snow was light and fluffy in character. Winds were also an issue, as sustained northerly speeds of generally 20-25 MPH and gusts to around 35 MPH promoted drifting snow and frequent visibility reductions to around one-half mile under the heaviest snow bands. The combination of winds and frigid temperatures dropped wind chill values into the -10 to -20 range from the late afternoon into the overnight hours. Snow first started pushing out of Kansas into southern portions of the area mainly between 7-9 AM, but the majority of impactful, accumulating snow within South Central Nebraska fell during the 11 AM-6 PM time frame, with only minor accumulations lingering into the night. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by a large, amplified trough that tracked northeast through the Central and Southern Plains. Given that the closed 700 millibar low pressure center tracked across the southern half of Kansas into Missouri, the local area was spared the highest snow totals of at least 9-12 that targeted parts of central and northeast Kansas. At the surface, a pronounced Arctic cold front plunged southward through South Central Nebraska during the morning hours, ushering in the frigid temperatures and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 8.0 inches fell in the community of Hubbell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was advancing east across central Nebraska around dawn on this Tuesday. Low pressure was along the front over Kansas and was moving east. The front continued eastward through the daytime hours and was at the Iowa border by sunset. The Kansas low moved to the Kansas City area. A broad upper level trough was over the United States, with a potent embedded shortwave trough advancing through the Intermountain West. At the base of this trough, a 120 knot upper level jet streak was ejecting into the Southern Plains. This put the Central Plains in the favorable quadrant for strong and deep upward motion. This system did not have much moisture to work with, so the amount of snow falling around the mid-level portion of the system over western Nebraska was paltry during the previous night and early morning hours. By mid-morning, however, a snow band began organizing from McCook to North Platte to Ainsworth. This snow band increased in size and intensity and advanced into south central Nebraska, appearing to curl around the back side of the mid-level circulation.||Generally, 2 to 6 inches of snow fell along and west of Highway 183. The highest amounts fell along and west of Highway 283. From 11 a.m. to 2 p.m., heavy snow fell over Furnas and western Dawson counties, along the southern edge of the band. The high snowfall rates and winds resulted in near blizzard conditions. Visibilities were reported near zero over western Dawson County. North winds with sustained speeds around 30 mph were recorded by the automated sites located at the Lexington and Holdrege airports, with peak gusts between 40 and 45 mph. The highest snowfall totals were in and around Gothenburg. A precipitation observer for the state of Nebraska reported over 12 inches, and an article from the Gothenburg Times mentioned 13 inches. The intensity of the snowband rapidly diminished after 2 p.m., as it advanced further across south central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest storm total snowfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county. A NeRAIN Observer in Miller also reported 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very windy conditions occurred over south central Nebraska on this Wednesday, especially east of Highway 183. High pressure was sprawled over the eastern United States, with a cold front advancing through the Intermountain West. Along this front, low pressure ejected from Wyoming into South Dakota. The result was a tight pressure gradient over the Central Plains. Unlike most high wind events, which occur with north winds, the high winds on this day were from the south. Numerous wind gusts over 50 mph were measured. Three locations, including Wood River, Shelby, and Glenvil measured peak gusts of 58 mph during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet site located 6 miles south of Glenvil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved into the central Plains during the day on Sunday April 27th. This system brought more widespread severe weather to areas of the southern Plains into the southeast United States, but for eastern Nebraska morning showers and thunderstorms, some of which were strong, severely limited the degree of instability that was available during the afternoon when a deep area of low pressure moved into central Nebraska. As a result only isolated thunderstorms were able to develop, and mainly confined to areas of central into northeast Nebraska where isolated severe weather in the form of large hail occurred. With the storm system still in the area on Monday the 28th, isolated strong storms again developed in the afternoon producing small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground was covered with mostly pea size hail, but a few up to quarter size was also observed in Pierce. The hail extended north of Pierce for several miles were hail up to half dollar size was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong arctic high pressure was over the Canadian Prairies on this Saturday, and its southern expanse encompassed most of the central United States. After a couple mild days in the 50s and 60s, temperatures plummeted. The surface weather maps changed little through the course of the day, and in the upper levels, flow was zonal over much of the country. An embedded low amplitude shortwave trough advanced from the Intermountain West onto the Central Plains by evening. A 110 knot upper level jet streak preceded this trough and by evening had shifted eastward enough that its entrance region became located over the Central Plains, creating a favorable environment for deep upward motion.||Around midday, a narrow (roughly one county wide) band of light snow developed along and just south of highway 6. The band initially developed over Gosper, Phelps, and Kearney counties, and then sagged into Furnas, Harlan, and Franklin counties through the mid- and late-afternoon hours. By 7 pm, the band had progressed across the state border into Kansas. Three to 5 inches of snow fell across portions of Furnas and Gosper counties, with lesser amounts in neighboring counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall averaged 2 to 4 inches across the county.

In light of the normal to above normal precipitation, several counties remained in severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions over the central and western Sandhills...as well as southwestern Nebraska.  The area of severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought remained unchanged during the month of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to portions of central and north central Nebraska  on March 18, 2014. As the storm system moved east heavy snow bands developed that led to snowfall amounts from 6 to 9 inches in portions of Rock, Blaine, Loup, and Custer counties. The banded snowfall created locally higher snow amounts to 12 inches in western Custer County to include the communities of Arnold and Callaway. Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusty, created significant blowing and drifting snow that led to near whiteouts and drifting snow during the afternoon hours. The combination of blowing and drifting resulted in the closure of Highway 92 from Arnold east to Merna. One fatality occurred indirectly from a rear end collision, where a 57 year old male died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow to the southwest half of Blaine County during the late morning and afternoon hours on March 18th, 2014. Law enforcement in Brewster total snowfall of 8 to 10 inches with snow drifts up to one and a half feet. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches were reported across the southwest half of Blaine County. Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusty caused poor visibility and considerable blowing and drifting snow.

Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusty caused poor visibility from considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One long-tracked supercell thunderstorm, and several other shorter-lived storms spawned tornadoes across eastern Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 11th.  The storms developed near the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in south central Nebraska then tracked northeast along the warm front toward the Lincoln and Omaha areas.   Conditions in the area were very unstable with temperatures in the 80s to around 90 south of the warm front with dew point temperatures in the 60s.  In total 12 tornadoes were recorded in the Omaha/Valley warning area with several others in the Hastings forecast area. The storms also produced very heavy rainfall which caused some flooding, mostly north through west and southwest of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding briefly closed the intersection of County Rd 14 and Highway 66.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the evening hours of Saturday the 22nd, peaking in intensity overnight and gradually moving out by late morning on the 23rd, a notably-narrow band of heavy snow in the 6-10 inch range targeted mainly the southwestern-most counties of South Central Nebraska. Meanwhile, the majority of the 24-county area outside this heavy banding observed little to no accumulation. The primary snow band of 6+ inches generally extended no more than 10 miles either side of a line stretched from Cambridge to Harlan County Lake, meaning that Furnas, Harlan and southern Gosper counties bore the brunt of the action. The highest unofficial snow total of 10 originated from Stamford, while a few of the highest NWS cooperative observer reports included 8.5 at Cambridge, 8 at Edison and 7 in Beaver City. Only a short distance north-northeast of the primary band, the Holdrege and Minden areas observed no more than 1-2. Fortunately, winds were not a major issue during this event, with sustained northeasterly speeds of generally 10-15 MPH keeping blowing and drifting to a minimum. ||Timing-wise, this quasi-stationary heavy snow band first started organizing over the area between 7-9 PM, peaked in intensity during the overnight hours, and gradually weakened and slid off to the east during the post-sunrise hours on the morning of the 23rd. Not surprisingly, the meteorological culprit for this narrow, banded snow event was a concentrated zone of mid level frontogenesis evident at the 700 millibar level, driven by lift from passing upper level jet streaks. On the larger scale aloft, a broad, low-amplitude trough encompassed most of the nation. At the surface during this snow event, South Central Nebraska became increasingly under the influence of high pressure building southward out of southern Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 6.0 inches fell 8 miles south of the community of Elwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From southwest Nebraska into eastern portions of north central Nebraska, much below normal precipitation ranging from a inch to locally more than two inches occurred for the month of April. The area of Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions remained the same across much of southwest Nebraska into the central Sandhills to end the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered. Severe (D2) drought conditions continued into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, February 2014 marked the 21st consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued some portion of this 24-county South Central Nebraska area, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. For most of the area, February marked the third or fourth consecutive month of below normal precipitation. According to reports from nearly 50 NWS cooperative observers, roughly 75 percent of South Central Nebraska measured at least slightly below normal February precipitation, and roughly 20% of the area (mainly north of Interstate 80) totaled less than half of normal February precipitation, based on long-term February normals generally ranging between 0.50 and 0.75. Despite the notably dry meteorological winter across most of the area, the weekly U.S. Drought Monitor continued to reflect generally minor changes in drought classification,  thus maintaining a west (worse) to east (better) disparity. Over the course of February, there was actually one small area of ���improvement���, as portions of central Harlan, southern Franklin and far southwestern Webster counties changed from category D2 Severe Drought to D1 Moderate Drought. However, farther north, a degradation from D0 Abnormally Dry (considered to be one category better than ���true��� drought) to D1 Moderate Drought overtook much of Greeley, northern Nance and far northern Howard counties. Meanwhile, D2 Severe Drought continued to plague all or part of a few southwestern counties at month's end, namely Furnas, Gosper, southern Dawson and southern Harlan. Just to the east and northeast of the D2 area, a corridor of D1 Moderate Drought covered all or most of several counties, including Valley, Sherman, Greeley, Phelps, Kearney, Franklin and Webster. Finally, several counties along and especially east of the Highway 281 corridor remained in D0 Abnormally Dry classification throughout February (including all of Hall, Adams, Hamilton, York and Thayer counties, among others). ||The majority (roughly two-thirds) of cooperative observer stations within South Central Nebraska reported February precipitation totals somewhere between 0.30-0.80���, although there was an evident northeast (drier, less snow) to southwest (wetter, snowier) disparity, mainly because the month���s two main snow events primarily targeted locations south of Highway 6. As for monthly extremes, a few of the lowest amounts included 0.12��� at Greeley, 0.13��� at Geneva and 0.19��� at both Belgrade and Ord airport. On the comparably ���wetter��� side of the spectrum were southern locations such as Wilsonville (1.05���), two miles northeast of Upland (1.03���), Edison (0.98) and four miles southwest of Blue Hill (0.98). Turning to the longer-term precipitation picture for all of meteorological winter (December-February), the 2013-2014 season was notably dry, especially within the northern three-fourths of South Central Nebraska. At Greeley, the paltry seasonal total of 0.22��� resulted in the 2nd-driest winter on record out of the 72 that have complete data. In the Tri-Cities, Grand Island notched the 9th-driest winter on record out of 119 seasons, while Hastings had the 11th-driest winter out of the 92 seasons with complete data. According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of February, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies remained relatively unchanged since late-January, checking in 57% short to very short and only 43% adequate. Subsoil moisture supplies also remained similar, rating 55% short to very short and 45% adequate.

In March significant snow fell in narrow bands that led to below normal precipitations for portions of the northern sandhills, southwest and up to 2 inches below normal for areas east of a line from Spencer to Merna. The areas of below normal precipitation led to continued Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions in the west, yet also caused an expansion of drought conditions into portions of north central Nebraska to include portions of Blaine, Loup, Garfield, and Custer counties as Moderate (D1) conditions worsened into Severe (D2) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 lingered. Severe (D2) drought conditions continued into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, March 2014 marked the 22nd-consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued some portion of this 24-county South Central Nebraska area, continuing one of the most notable droughts in decades. For most of the area, March was at least the fifth-consecutive month of below normal precipitation, but compared to 30-year normals it was hands-down the driest month of all recent ones. According to reports from nearly 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also taking into account radar-derived precipitation estimates, all of South Central Nebraska measured below normal March precipitation (based on long-term March normals of roughly 1.85). However, more significantly, nearly 90% of the area received less than one-fourth of normal March precipitation. Given these dubious March precipitation trends, the weekly U.S. Drought Monitor reflected a one-category degradation in drought classification across much of the 24-county area. At month's end, roughly 75% of South Central Nebraska was either in category D1 Moderate Drought or D2 Severe Drought. The D1 Moderate Drought area included several counties along and west of Highway 281, along with Nance and Merrick counties and portions of Nuckolls, Thayer and Polk counties farther east. Meanwhile, worse D2 Severe Drought continued to plague all or parts of western counties such as Furnas, Gosper, Dawson, Harlan and Phelps, and now included most of Valley County as well. Farther east, all of Hamilton, York, Clay and Fillmore counties along with at least small portions of several bordering counties continued to fare a bit better with D0 Abnormally Dry classification (considered to be one category better than ���true��� drought). ||Not surprisingly, many locations within the area endured one of the Top-10 driest months of March on record, including Grand Island airport with its 9th-driest out of 117 years with only 0.19, and Hastings airport which also had its 9th-driest out of 115 years with merely 0.15. Even more impressively, Red Cloud tied with a handful of previous years for the outright-driest March on record out of 118 years with only a paltry trace. Although still plenty dry, there was one localized exception within the area that received around one-half to as much as two-thirds of normal March precipitation: mainly portions of Dawson, Gosper and Furnas counties. In those counties, most precipitation fell on the 18th and/or 22nd in the form of several inches of snow. As for monthly extremes, a few of the lowest March totals per NWS cooperative observers included only a trace at Red Cloud and Bruning, 0.03 at Hebron and 0.05 at Hubbell. On the comparably ���wetter��� side of the spectrum were western stations including Gothenburg (1.30���), Cambridge (0.81���) and eight miles south of Elwood (0.73). According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of March, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies degraded slightly since late-February, as the short to very short categories increased from 57% to 62%. Subsoil moisture supplies followed a similar trend, degrading from 55% to 60% short to very short.

Farther east, all of Hamilton, York, Clay and Fillmore counties along with at least small portions of several bordering counties continued to fare a bit better with D0 Abnormally Dry classification (considered to be one category better than ���true��� drought). ||Not surprisingly, many locations within the area endured one of the Top-10 driest months of March on record, including Grand Island airport with its 9th-driest out of 117 years with only 0.19, and Hastings airport which also had its 9th-driest out of 115 years with merely 0.15. Even more impressively, Red Cloud tied with a handful of previous years for the outright-driest March on record out of 118 years with only a paltry trace. Although still plenty dry, there was one localized exception within the area that received around one-half to as much as two-thirds of normal March precipitation: mainly portions of Dawson, Gosper and Furnas counties. In those counties, most precipitation fell on the 18th and/or 22nd in the form of several inches of snow. As for monthly extremes, a few of the lowest March totals per NWS cooperative observers included only a trace at Red Cloud and Bruning, 0.03 at Hebron and 0.05 at Hubbell. On the comparably ���wetter��� side of the spectrum were western stations including Gothenburg (1.30���), Cambridge (0.81���) and eight miles south of Elwood (0.73). According to the USDA Nebraska Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of March, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies degraded slightly since late-February, as the short to very short categories increased from 57% to 62%. Subsoil moisture supplies followed a similar trend, degrading from 55% to 60% short to very short.

Isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms raked across portions of South Central Nebraska on this Sunday afternoon, resulting in not only several reports of large hail and damaging winds, but also Nebraska's first three tornadoes of 2014. Fortunately, these tornadoes were of the weak and brief variety (rated EF-0) and resulted in no reports of damage. The first tornado touched down shortly before 230 PM CDT in far northern Franklin County near Upland. The other two tornadoes occurred a few hours later between 430-530 PM, associated with the strongest, most organized supercell storm of the day as it tracked north through the far eastern counties of the local area. One of these tornadoes touched down north of Bradshaw in York County and the other in west-central Polk County several miles west-northwest of Osceola. As for hail reports, the majority of them originated from western Thayer and Fillmore and northern Adams counties, including some stones up to golf ball size. There were only a few reports of marginally severe winds, highlighted by a measured 62 MPH gust near Wood River. ||All strong to severe thunderstorms within the 24-county area occurred between 12-6 PM CDT in association with a seasonably classic and powerful, vertically-stacked low pressure system migrating northeastward into South Central Nebraska. All severe storms also concentrated east and northeast of a Lexington-Franklin line, as the far southwestern counties were spared due to the passage of a well-defined, northward-drifting surface dryline and occluding cold front. There were essentially two primary areas and modes of severe storm development. The first, which accounted for most of the day's thunderstorm activity, consisted of a broken arc of storms lifting north across mainly central and west-central portions of the area. Several individual cells within this arc drifted northwestward in sync with the counter-clockwise circulation of the parent low. The initial development got going over Dawson County between 12-1 PM CDT, but these storms yielded no reports of severe weather before departing the local area into Custer and Lincoln counties. Following a brief lull, the second and more dominant arc of convection flared up generally along an axis running from eastern Dawson to Webster counties between 130 PM and 3 PM CDT, during which time the first tornado occurred near Upland. Eventually, the eastern-most cells in the arc became the most intense as they lifted north-northwest, resulting in several hail reports along a path through counties including Adams, Hall and Sherman between 330 PM and 5 PM CDT before weakening and eventually departing the local area to the north. Meanwhile, the second primary area/mode of severe weather developed farther east, and revolved around an isolated supercell that developed over southwestern Thayer County around 230 PM CDT and then drifted almost due north for the next several hours, finally departing the local area out of far eastern Nance and Merrick counties around 6 PM CDT. This lone supercell produced both the York and Polk County tornadoes, along with several hail reports. Just to the east of this main supercell of the day, another briefly-severe storm affected far eastern York County around 5 PM CDT.  All strong-severe convection vacated the entire 24-county area by around 6 PM CDT, leaving behind only lingering stratiform rain in western counties under the core of the mid level low.||On the synoptic scale, the day began with a deep, vertically stacked closed low centered over central portions of the Kansas-Colorado border area as its 984 millibar surface low started to occlude. By early afternoon, severe storm development in South Central Nebraska focused within a corridor of steep low level lapse rates under the mid-level dry slot, with the southwestern extent of storm development defined by the leading edge of the northward-drifting occluded front/dryline. By late afternoon, the deep low had migrated to near McCook, allowing the surface front to push northward through all of South Central and ending the severe threat. Ahead of this occluded front/sharp dryline, surface-based CAPE climbed to around 1000 J/kg in an environment featuring surface dewpoints in the mid-upper 50s F. Behind the boundary, dewpoints quickly crashed into the 30s F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy pea size hail occurred along with estimated 60 mph winds occurred at mile marker 359 on Interstate 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe weather that occurred across South Central Nebraska, this Wednesday evening, was produced by a lone supercell storm that tracked east-northeast across Dawson, Buffalo, Sherman, Howard, and Greeley counties. The core of the storm entered southwestern Dawson county just after 7 p.m. CDT. The storm produced hail up to the size of baseballs in Lexington, as well as a funnel cloud 4 miles south of the city. Wind damage was also reported nearby. As the storm tracked into less populated areas, it continue to produce hail up to the size of golf balls. Another funnel cloud occurred 6 miles north of Amherst in Buffalo county. Significant wind damage occurred near the town of Hazard, with a pole barn destroyed, several utility poles snapped off and debris transported from southwest to northeast. The storm gradually weakened as it moved into Howard and Greeley counties. However, it still downed several trees as far east as Elba before its rapid demise thereafter. ||The synoptic situation was complex. A quasi-stationary front extended from the Ohio Valley westward to low pressure near Sioux Falls, Iowa, then southwestward across Nebraska, Kansas, and Colorado. A new cold front was moving in and attempting to merge with the stationary front near the time this storm developed. At the upper-levels, a deep trough was over the western U.S. with a ridge over the east. MLCAPE was around 1500 J/kg and deep layer shear was near 60 knots, creating a favorable environment for the supercell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 70 MPH. Reports from the area included damage to an irrigation well engine shed and a power pole knocked down approximately 1 mile east of Lexington and one mile north of Interstate 80. A large cottonwood tree was also knocked down east of Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across eastern Colorado along a frontal zone and moved  northeast into western Nebraska. The storm produced up hail up to 1.75 inches in Garden County. As the storm pushed east the wind threat increased, where winds to 80 mph produced storm damage in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 60 mph was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms formed along and southeast of a line from Red Cloud to York on this Sunday afternoon. However, most of the severe weather was produced by a single supercell. This was the first and most intense storm of the day and it formed over Webster county around 2:30 p.m. CDT. This storm took full advantage of an extremely favorable environment, quickly becoming severe and producing at least one brief, weak tornado just southwest of Glenvil. The storm continued northeast producing several more tornadoes, two of which resulted in a maximum damage rating of EF-3. One of these tornadoes occurred over Clay and Fillmore counties and the other subsequently occurred over Fillmore and Seward counties. The first of these EF-3 tornadoes was on the ground for nearly 22 miles. Damage surveys, photos and video confirmed that this storm produced at least 6 other tornadoes, 3 rated EF-0, 2 rated EF-1 and 1 rated EF-2. One of the EF-1 tornadoes was anticyclonic and touched down just north of Fairmont. To the left of these tornadoes, severe hail was common. The largest hail observed was the size of tennis balls in Glenvil. Large swaths of damaging winds, from the rear flank downdraft, accompanied the tornadoes to their south and southeast. Tornado and wind damage was extensive to trees, power poles, farm buildings including grain bins, and a few homes. Over 200 irrigation pivots were overturned and/or twisted. Multiple farmsteads also endured damage, including the loss of a new  machine shed. By 5:30 p.m., this storm had moved into eastern Nebraska. Other storms developed in its wake, but produced no severe weather. ||A very long surface front extended from New England, through the Ohio Valley, across the Central Plains and into the Desert Southwest. Portions of this front were stationary while others were being modulated by several waves of low pressure. The front lifted northward to the Kansas-Nebraska border by daybreak. One low ejected out of Colorado and moved east along the front through northern Kansas during the day. This supercell formed just northeast of this low on the immediate cool side of the front. The upper-level flow featured an amplified western trough and eastern ridge. A closed low was at the base of the trough over the Four Corners region. These storms initiated with the aid of a shortwave trough that ejected into the Central Plains ahead of this low. A strong 100 knot jet streak was at the base of the trough at 300 mb, placing the Central Plains in the favorable left front quadrant. An expansive stratus cloud deck, on the cool side of the front, resulted in a substantial temperature gradient across the front, with afternoon temperatures in the 40s and 50s across much of Nebraska to the mid 80s and low 90s over northern Kansas. This resulted in a tight instability gradient with MLCAPE of 2000 J/kg right along the front. Winds were strong throughout the column. The ejecting low pressure center, combined with the front, resulted in significantly backed winds, enhancing low-level shear. 0-1 km SRH was roughly 300 m2/s2, with 0-3 km SRH 400-600 m2/s2. The environment was very favorable for severe storms and tornadoes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm produced a wet downburst in Perkins County around 11 PM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet downburst caused damage to one business, destroyed a small greenhouse and rolled over a semi truck trailer. Two pickup trucks also sustained damage. The business had two tall garage doors destroyed with two windows broken and one side door damaged as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed in Holt County that produced large hail.
An isolated severe thunderstorm developed in Holt County that produced large hail.
An isolated severe thunderstorm developed in Holt County that produced large hail.

A line of storms that developed across eastern Colorado and the Nebraska panhandle moved into western Nebraska. The storms weakened while moving east with an isolated severe episode that produced large hail.

The drought that began in June 2012 was eased across the northwest as convection brought rainfall. Further south and east, the drought intensified into portions of the sandhills and north central to Extreme (D3) conditions and Severe (D2) conditions northeast into eastern Holt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 changed little during May. Severe (D2) drought conditions lingered through all but the extreme north. The northwest portion of the county saw around 2 inches of rainfall for the month. The drought continued into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Wednesday afternoon and evening featured the first multiple severe thunderstorm event of the spring season in South Central Nebraska, as an axis of heavy rainfall and a smattering of severe-criteria hail reports concentrated within a narrow corridor near a slow-moving cold front. The most-active weather focused within a roughly 30-mile wide axis extending from Harlan and Franklin counties in the southwest, then northeast through the Minden, Hastings, Grand Island, Aurora and Osceola areas.  Most hail was only up to nickel or quarter size, including several reports from the vicinity of Juniata, Hastings, Ragan, Trumbull and Kenesaw. However, larger hail to golf ball size was reported in Franklin, and two-inch diameter hail fell near Prosser. On a positive note, many of the same areas that observed hail also received soaking rainfall of at least 1-3 inches, which was much-needed in the wake of a rather dry winter and early spring. A few of the highest storm total amounts from NeRAIN observers featured 4.15 two miles west of Doniphan and 3.13 four miles of Aurora. Unfortunately, some portions of the 24-county area missed out on decent rainfall, as locations primarily northwest of a line from Beaver City-Ravenna-Greeley only averaged around 0.40 or less and parts of Gosper and Dawson counties barely saw a drop. ||As for event-timing, initially semi-isolated strong to severe storms flared up within the aforementioned narrow frontal zone between 330-430 pm CDT. For the next several hours, multiple storms trained along this same general corridor, exhibiting multicell and brief supercell structures as they congealed into a nearly solid line. Between 730-830 pm CDT, this activity steadily weakened below severe thresholds, largely due to the onset of diurnal cooling and the increasingly outflow-dominant mode. As storms weakened around sunset, the expanding outflow boundaries allowed for increasing coverage of generally weak convection across the majority of South Central Nebraska, with light to moderate rainfall lingering well into the morning hours of the 24th. In the mid-upper levels, the primary forcing for this event was provided by low-amplitude shortwave troughs kicking out across the Central Plains in southwesterly flow, downstream from a large-scale trough axis slowly sliding east through the Rockies. At the surface, the aforementioned, well-defined cold front was the primary low-level trigger, as this front stretched from northeast Nebraska through northwest Kansas during the late afternoon where it connected to a strong 993 millibar low centered in far eastern Colorado. Although low-level moisture was seasonably sparse with surface dewpoints only averaging in the upper 40s-low 50s F, steep low-mid level lapse rates promoted modest mixed-layer CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of respectable 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 40-50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Wednesday afternoon and evening featured the first multiple severe thunderstorm event of the spring season in South Central Nebraska, as an axis of heavy rainfall and a smattering of severe-criteria hail reports concentrated within a narrow corridor near a slow-moving cold front. The most-active weather focused within a roughly 30-mile wide axis extending from Harlan and Franklin counties in the southwest, then northeast through the Minden, Hastings, Grand Island, Aurora and Osceola areas.  Most hail was only up to nickel or quarter size, including several reports from the vicinity of Juniata, Hastings, Ragan, Trumbull and Kenesaw. However, larger hail to golf ball size was reported in Franklin, and two-inch diameter hail fell near Prosser. On a positive note, many of the same areas that observed hail also received soaking rainfall of at least 1-3 inches, which was much-needed in the wake of a rather dry winter and early spring. A few of the highest storm total amounts from NeRAIN observers featured 4.15 two miles west of Doniphan and 3.13 four miles of Aurora. Unfortunately, some portions of the 24-county area missed out on decent rainfall, as locations primarily northwest of a line from Beaver City-Ravenna-Greeley only averaged around 0.40 or less and parts of Gosper and Dawson counties barely saw a drop. ||As for event-timing, initially semi-isolated strong to severe storms flared up within the aforementioned narrow frontal zone between 330-430 pm CDT. For the next several hours, multiple storms trained along this same general corridor, exhibiting multicell and brief supercell structures as they congealed into a nearly solid line. Between 730-830 pm CDT, this activity steadily weakened below severe thresholds, largely due to the onset of diurnal cooling and the increasingly outflow-dominant mode. As storms weakened around sunset, the expanding outflow boundaries allowed for increasing coverage of generally weak convection across the majority of South Central Nebraska, with light to moderate rainfall lingering well into the morning hours of the 24th. In the mid-upper levels, the primary forcing for this event was provided by low-amplitude shortwave troughs kicking out across the Central Plains in southwesterly flow, downstream from a large-scale trough axis slowly sliding east through the Rockies. At the surface, the aforementioned, well-defined cold front was the primary low-level trigger, as this front stretched from northeast Nebraska through northwest Kansas during the late afternoon where it connected to a strong 993 millibar low centered in far eastern Colorado. Although low-level moisture was seasonably sparse with surface dewpoints only averaging in the upper 40s-low 50s F, steep low-mid level lapse rates promoted modest mixed-layer CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of respectable 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 40-50 knots.

Multiple thunderstorms formed along and southeast of a line from Red Cloud to York on this Sunday afternoon. However, most of the severe weather was produced by a single supercell. This was the first and most intense storm of the day and it formed over Webster county around 2:30 p.m. CDT. This storm took full advantage of an extremely favorable environment, quickly becoming severe and producing at least one brief, weak tornado just southwest of Glenvil. The storm continued northeast producing several more tornadoes, two of which resulted in a maximum damage rating of EF-3. One of these tornadoes occurred over Clay and Fillmore counties and the other subsequently occurred over Fillmore and Seward counties. The first of these EF-3 tornadoes was on the ground for nearly 22 miles. Damage surveys, photos and video confirmed that this storm produced at least 6 other tornadoes, 3 rated EF-0, 2 rated EF-1 and 1 rated EF-2. One of the EF-1 tornadoes was anticyclonic and touched down just north of Fairmont. To the left of these tornadoes, severe hail was common. The largest hail observed was the size of tennis balls in Glenvil. Large swaths of damaging winds, from the rear flank downdraft, accompanied the tornadoes to their south and southeast. Tornado and wind damage was extensive to trees, power poles, farm buildings including grain bins, and a few homes. Over 200 irrigation pivots were overturned and/or twisted. Multiple farmsteads also endured damage, including the loss of a new  machine shed. By 5:30 p.m., this storm had moved into eastern Nebraska. Other storms developed in its wake, but produced no severe weather. ||A very long surface front extended from New England, through the Ohio Valley, across the Central Plains and into the Desert Southwest. Portions of this front were stationary while others were being modulated by several waves of low pressure. The front lifted northward to the Kansas-Nebraska border by daybreak. One low ejected out of Colorado and moved east along the front through northern Kansas during the day. This supercell formed just northeast of this low on the immediate cool side of the front. The upper-level flow featured an amplified western trough and eastern ridge. A closed low was at the base of the trough over the Four Corners region. These storms initiated with the aid of a shortwave trough that ejected into the Central Plains ahead of this low. A strong 100 knot jet streak was at the base of the trough at 300 mb, placing the Central Plains in the favorable left front quadrant. An expansive stratus cloud deck, on the cool side of the front, resulted in a substantial temperature gradient across the front, with afternoon temperatures in the 40s and 50s across much of Nebraska to the mid 80s and low 90s over northern Kansas. This resulted in a tight instability gradient with MLCAPE of 2000 J/kg right along the front. Winds were strong throughout the column. The ejecting low pressure center, combined with the front, resulted in significantly backed winds, enhancing low-level shear. 0-1 km SRH was roughly 300 m2/s2, with 0-3 km SRH 400-600 m2/s2. The environment was very favorable for severe storms and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 24 hour rainfall total reported from Sunday through Monday morning was 3.08 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was advancing east across central Nebraska around dawn on this Tuesday. Low pressure was along the front over Kansas and was moving east. The front continued eastward through the daytime hours and was at the Iowa border by sunset. The Kansas low moved to the Kansas City area. A broad upper level trough was over the United States, with a potent embedded shortwave trough advancing through the Intermountain West. At the base of this trough, a 120 knot upper level jet streak was ejecting into the Southern Plains. This put the Central Plains in the favorable quadrant for strong and deep upward motion. This system did not have much moisture to work with, so the amount of snow falling around the mid-level portion of the system over western Nebraska was paltry during the previous night and early morning hours. By mid-morning, however, a snow band began organizing from McCook to North Platte to Ainsworth. This snow band increased in size and intensity and advanced into south central Nebraska, appearing to curl around the back side of the mid-level circulation.||Generally, 2 to 6 inches of snow fell along and west of Highway 183. The highest amounts fell along and west of Highway 283. From 11 a.m. to 2 p.m., heavy snow fell over Furnas and western Dawson counties, along the southern edge of the band. The high snowfall rates and winds resulted in near blizzard conditions. Visibilities were reported near zero over western Dawson County. North winds with sustained speeds around 30 mph were recorded by the automated sites located at the Lexington and Holdrege airports, with peak gusts between 40 and 45 mph. The highest snowfall totals were in and around Gothenburg. A precipitation observer for the state of Nebraska reported over 12 inches, and an article from the Gothenburg Times mentioned 13 inches. The intensity of the snowband rapidly diminished after 2 p.m., as it advanced further across south central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest storm total snowfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county. A NWS Cooperative Observer in Minden reported 3.9 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very windy conditions occurred over south central Nebraska on this Wednesday, especially east of Highway 183. High pressure was sprawled over the eastern United States, with a cold front advancing through the Intermountain West. Along this front, low pressure ejected from Wyoming into South Dakota. The result was a tight pressure gradient over the Central Plains. Unlike most high wind events, which occur with north winds, the high winds on this day were from the south. Numerous wind gusts over 50 mph were measured. Three locations, including Wood River, Shelby, and Glenvil measured peak gusts of 58 mph during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet site located 1 mile south of Wood River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 was eased across the northwest as convection brought rainfall. Further south and east, the drought intensified into portions of the sandhills and north central to Extreme (D3) conditions and Severe (D2) conditions northeast into eastern Holt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 intensified across the north in May. Extreme (D3) drought conditions were seen across the north half of the county with Severe (D2) conditions in the south. The county received an average of 1 to 3 inches of rainfall with the greatest across the northwest. The drought continued into June.

The front continued eastward through the daytime hours and was at the Iowa border by sunset. The Kansas low moved to the Kansas City area. A broad upper level trough was over the United States, with a potent embedded shortwave trough advancing through the Intermountain West. At the base of this trough, a 120 knot upper level jet streak was ejecting into the Southern Plains. This put the Central Plains in the favorable quadrant for strong and deep upward motion. This system did not have much moisture to work with, so the amount of snow falling around the mid-level portion of the system over western Nebraska was paltry during the previous night and early morning hours. By mid-morning, however, a snow band began organizing from McCook to North Platte to Ainsworth. This snow band increased in size and intensity and advanced into south central Nebraska, appearing to curl around the back side of the mid-level circulation.||Generally, 2 to 6 inches of snow fell along and west of Highway 183. The highest amounts fell along and west of Highway 283. From 11 a.m. to 2 p.m., heavy snow fell over Furnas and western Dawson counties, along the southern edge of the band. The high snowfall rates and winds resulted in near blizzard conditions. Visibilities were reported near zero over western Dawson County. North winds with sustained speeds around 30 mph were recorded by the automated sites located at the Lexington and Holdrege airports, with peak gusts between 40 and 45 mph. The highest snowfall totals were in and around Gothenburg. A precipitation observer for the state of Nebraska reported over 12 inches, and an article from the Gothenburg Times mentioned 13 inches. The intensity of the snowband rapidly diminished after 2 p.m., as it advanced further across south central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest storm total snowfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county.  A NWS Cooperative Observer 2 miles northeast of Upland reported 3 inches of snow, as did a NeRAIN Observer 5 miles south-southeast of Hildreth.

This Wednesday afternoon and evening featured the first multiple severe thunderstorm event of the spring season in South Central Nebraska, as an axis of heavy rainfall and a smattering of severe-criteria hail reports concentrated within a narrow corridor near a slow-moving cold front. The most-active weather focused within a roughly 30-mile wide axis extending from Harlan and Franklin counties in the southwest, then northeast through the Minden, Hastings, Grand Island, Aurora and Osceola areas.  Most hail was only up to nickel or quarter size, including several reports from the vicinity of Juniata, Hastings, Ragan, Trumbull and Kenesaw. However, larger hail to golf ball size was reported in Franklin, and two-inch diameter hail fell near Prosser. On a positive note, many of the same areas that observed hail also received soaking rainfall of at least 1-3 inches, which was much-needed in the wake of a rather dry winter and early spring. A few of the highest storm total amounts from NeRAIN observers featured 4.15 two miles west of Doniphan and 3.13 four miles of Aurora. Unfortunately, some portions of the 24-county area missed out on decent rainfall, as locations primarily northwest of a line from Beaver City-Ravenna-Greeley only averaged around 0.40 or less and parts of Gosper and Dawson counties barely saw a drop. ||As for event-timing, initially semi-isolated strong to severe storms flared up within the aforementioned narrow frontal zone between 330-430 pm CDT. For the next several hours, multiple storms trained along this same general corridor, exhibiting multicell and brief supercell structures as they congealed into a nearly solid line. Between 730-830 pm CDT, this activity steadily weakened below severe thresholds, largely due to the onset of diurnal cooling and the increasingly outflow-dominant mode. As storms weakened around sunset, the expanding outflow boundaries allowed for increasing coverage of generally weak convection across the majority of South Central Nebraska, with light to moderate rainfall lingering well into the morning hours of the 24th. In the mid-upper levels, the primary forcing for this event was provided by low-amplitude shortwave troughs kicking out across the Central Plains in southwesterly flow, downstream from a large-scale trough axis slowly sliding east through the Rockies. At the surface, the aforementioned, well-defined cold front was the primary low-level trigger, as this front stretched from northeast Nebraska through northwest Kansas during the late afternoon where it connected to a strong 993 millibar low centered in far eastern Colorado. Although low-level moisture was seasonably sparse with surface dewpoints only averaging in the upper 40s-low 50s F, steep low-mid level lapse rates promoted modest mixed-layer CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of respectable 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 40-50 knots.

Multiple thunderstorms formed along and southeast of a line from Red Cloud to York on this Sunday afternoon. However, most of the severe weather was produced by a single supercell. This was the first and most intense storm of the day and it formed over Webster county around 2:30 p.m. CDT. This storm took full advantage of an extremely favorable environment, quickly becoming severe and producing at least one brief, weak tornado just southwest of Glenvil. The storm continued northeast producing several more tornadoes, two of which resulted in a maximum damage rating of EF-3. One of these tornadoes occurred over Clay and Fillmore counties and the other subsequently occurred over Fillmore and Seward counties. The first of these EF-3 tornadoes was on the ground for nearly 22 miles. Damage surveys, photos and video confirmed that this storm produced at least 6 other tornadoes, 3 rated EF-0, 2 rated EF-1 and 1 rated EF-2. One of the EF-1 tornadoes was anticyclonic and touched down just north of Fairmont. To the left of these tornadoes, severe hail was common. The largest hail observed was the size of tennis balls in Glenvil. Large swaths of damaging winds, from the rear flank downdraft, accompanied the tornadoes to their south and southeast. Tornado and wind damage was extensive to trees, power poles, farm buildings including grain bins, and a few homes. Over 200 irrigation pivots were overturned and/or twisted. Multiple farmsteads also endured damage, including the loss of a new  machine shed. By 5:30 p.m., this storm had moved into eastern Nebraska. Other storms developed in its wake, but produced no severe weather. ||A very long surface front extended from New England, through the Ohio Valley, across the Central Plains and into the Desert Southwest. Portions of this front were stationary while others were being modulated by several waves of low pressure. The front lifted northward to the Kansas-Nebraska border by daybreak. One low ejected out of Colorado and moved east along the front through northern Kansas during the day. This supercell formed just northeast of this low on the immediate cool side of the front. The upper-level flow featured an amplified western trough and eastern ridge. A closed low was at the base of the trough over the Four Corners region. These storms initiated with the aid of a shortwave trough that ejected into the Central Plains ahead of this low. A strong 100 knot jet streak was at the base of the trough at 300 mb, placing the Central Plains in the favorable left front quadrant. An expansive stratus cloud deck, on the cool side of the front, resulted in a substantial temperature gradient across the front, with afternoon temperatures in the 40s and 50s across much of Nebraska to the mid 80s and low 90s over northern Kansas. This resulted in a tight instability gradient with MLCAPE of 2000 J/kg right along the front. Winds were strong throughout the column. The ejecting low pressure center, combined with the front, resulted in significantly backed winds, enhancing low-level shear. 0-1 km SRH was roughly 300 m2/s2, with 0-3 km SRH 400-600 m2/s2. The environment was very favorable for severe storms and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado actually moved southwest and was eventually engulfed into the main Cordova tornado. No damage was reported with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One long-tracked supercell thunderstorm, and several other shorter-lived storms spawned tornadoes across eastern Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours of May 11th.  The storms developed near the intersection of a warm front and dry-line/cold front in south central Nebraska then tracked northeast along the warm front toward the Lincoln and Omaha areas.   Conditions in the area were very unstable with temperatures in the 80s to around 90 south of the warm front with dew point temperatures in the 60s.  In total 12 tornadoes were recorded in the Omaha/Valley warning area with several others in the Hastings forecast area. The storms also produced very heavy rainfall which caused some flooding, mostly north through west and southwest of Lincoln.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado crossed into Seward county from York county and was from a trailing supercell thunderstorm that followed the storm that produced an EF3 tornado just to the south a little earlier. This tornado was responsible snapping several power poles as it entered into Seward County along 476th street. The tornado continued northeast producing occasional tree damage, tipping over center pivot irrigation equipment, and outbuilding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the early morning hours on this Sunday and continuing well into the evening in eastern counties, a pronounced collision of spring and winter fueled a gamut of weather across South Central Nebraska. This included strong to marginally severe thunderstorms mainly in the southeast half of the 24-county area, near-blizzard conditions and several inches of wet, heavy snow mainly in the northwest half of the area, and also several hours of sustained north winds generally 30-35 MPH commonly gusting 40-50+ MPH. By far the biggest story of the day was the worse-than-expected combination of moderate to heavy snow and strong wind. Although travel became treacherous in several counties, a few of the most newsworthy issues included the closure of Interstate 80 between Kearney and Elm Creek and the opening of a warming shelter for stranded motorists in Holdrege. As for storm-total snowfall, the highest concentration of 3-6 amounts (and locally higher) occurred within a southwest-to northeast band centered roughly 15 miles either side of a line from near Beaver City-Holdrege-Ravenna-Wolbach. Within this hardest-hit zone, a few of the highest snow totals from NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers featured 7.5 three miles west of Elba, 7.0 seven miles south of Litchfield and also four miles east-southeast of Sumner, 6.7 at Wolbach and 6.1 at St. Paul. Outside this primary snow band, including most locations south and southeast of a line from Franklin-Grand Island-York, accumulations averaged no more than 1-2. However, even these areas endured at least a brief bout of very poor visibilities in wind-blown snow. As for the timing of snow, it was a typical west-to-east transition from rain to snow with a brief period of sleet in between. The aforementioned, significant snow band that targeted western and northern counties produced most of its impacts between 11 AM-3 PM CDT, remaining quasi-stationary for several hours within a focused mid-level deformation zone. During the later afternoon and early evening hours, the band became much more progressive as it marched eastward through the remainder of South Central Nebraska, eventually exiting the eastern fringes of the local area between 8-10 PM CDT. ||Before the wintry aspect of this system took center stage, convection was the initial focus of the day as primarily the southeast half of the 24-county area saw fairly widespread coverage of thunderstorms for the first time in 2014. Strong storms began flaring up mainly south of Interstate 80 between 6-8 AM CDT, with one storm producing hail up to quarter size in far eastern Fillmore County. Although no additional storms met severe-criteria, several produced copious amounts of small hail, resulting in a report of slick roadways in parts of Franklin County. Although the existing gradient wind across the area was already quite strong, the presence of thunderstorm outflow likely further-enhanced gusts, resulting in peak speeds to around 60 MPH as measured at the Kearney airport. Thunderstorm-enhanced winds likely also contributed to the destruction of a building under construction in eastern Franklin County. As increasingly-colder air surged across the area from west-to-east during the late morning and early afternoon hours, thunderstorms all but ended within the area by 2 PM CDT. ||As earlier mentioned, this snow event ended up being much more impactful than anticipated, in part because most computer forecast models failed to predict the intense banding and also underestimated the speed and magnitude of cold air surging into the area. Furthermore, the heavy snowfall rates overwhelmed any melting influences from antecedent warm ground conditions. As testament to the stark weather changes associated with this system, afternoon high temperatures on the preceding afternoon of the 12th ranged from 76-80 F across most of South Central Nebraska. By daybreak on the 13th, the powerful cold front had pushed well south of the area along an axis from southwest Kansas to southern Iowa, with temperatures across most of the local area already well down into the 40s F. By early afternoon when snow was well-underway, temperatures across the majority of South Central Nebraska had plunged to 30-35 F, promoting wind chill values generally 15-20 F. In the mid-upper levels, a longwave trough dominated much of the central United States and Nebraska resided under a favorable coupled upper jet regime within the exit region of a southern stream branch sliding across the Southern Plains and the entrance region of a northern stream jet centered from the eastern Dakotas into Canada. A bit lower in the troposphere, a pronounced zone of frontogenesis/deformation focused across the heart of South Central Nebraska along the path of a 700 millibar low pressure center and to the northwest of a closed 850 millibar low that tracked from southwest Kansas to southern Iowa. On a positive note, the combination of rain and snow brought much-needed liquid precipitation totals of 0.40-0.80 to the majority of the area, with amounts in excess of 1 common in the corridor of heaviest snow, highlighted by 1.98 at Holdrege. As is typical of April snow, it did not stick around long as high temperatures two days later bounced back into the 60s.

Within this hardest-hit zone, a few of the highest snow totals from NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers featured 7.5 three miles west of Elba, 7.0 seven miles south of Litchfield and also four miles east-southeast of Sumner, 6.7 at Wolbach and 6.1 at St. Paul. Outside this primary snow band, including most locations south and southeast of a line from Franklin-Grand Island-York, accumulations averaged no more than 1-2. However, even these areas endured at least a brief bout of very poor visibilities in wind-blown snow. As for the timing of snow, it was a typical west-to-east transition from rain to snow with a brief period of sleet in between. The aforementioned, significant snow band that targeted western and northern counties produced most of its impacts between 11 AM-3 PM CDT, remaining quasi-stationary for several hours within a focused mid-level deformation zone. During the later afternoon and early evening hours, the band became much more progressive as it marched eastward through the remainder of South Central Nebraska, eventually exiting the eastern fringes of the local area between 8-10 PM CDT. ||Before the wintry aspect of this system took center stage, convection was the initial focus of the day as primarily the southeast half of the 24-county area saw fairly widespread coverage of thunderstorms for the first time in 2014. Strong storms began flaring up mainly south of Interstate 80 between 6-8 AM CDT, with one storm producing hail up to quarter size in far eastern Fillmore County. Although no additional storms met severe-criteria, several produced copious amounts of small hail, resulting in a report of slick roadways in parts of Franklin County. Although the existing gradient wind across the area was already quite strong, the presence of thunderstorm outflow likely further-enhanced gusts, resulting in peak speeds to around 60 MPH as measured at the Kearney airport. Thunderstorm-enhanced winds likely also contributed to the destruction of a building under construction in eastern Franklin County. As increasingly-colder air surged across the area from west-to-east during the late morning and early afternoon hours, thunderstorms all but ended within the area by 2 PM CDT. ||As earlier mentioned, this snow event ended up being much more impactful than anticipated, in part because most computer forecast models failed to predict the intense banding and also underestimated the speed and magnitude of cold air surging into the area. Furthermore, the heavy snowfall rates overwhelmed any melting influences from antecedent warm ground conditions. As testament to the stark weather changes associated with this system, afternoon high temperatures on the preceding afternoon of the 12th ranged from 76-80 F across most of South Central Nebraska. By daybreak on the 13th, the powerful cold front had pushed well south of the area along an axis from southwest Kansas to southern Iowa, with temperatures across most of the local area already well down into the 40s F. By early afternoon when snow was well-underway, temperatures across the majority of South Central Nebraska had plunged to 30-35 F, promoting wind chill values generally 15-20 F. In the mid-upper levels, a longwave trough dominated much of the central United States and Nebraska resided under a favorable coupled upper jet regime within the exit region of a southern stream branch sliding across the Southern Plains and the entrance region of a northern stream jet centered from the eastern Dakotas into Canada. A bit lower in the troposphere, a pronounced zone of frontogenesis/deformation focused across the heart of South Central Nebraska along the path of a 700 millibar low pressure center and to the northwest of a closed 850 millibar low that tracked from southwest Kansas to southern Iowa. On a positive note, the combination of rain and snow brought much-needed liquid precipitation totals of 0.40-0.80 to the majority of the area, with amounts in excess of 1 common in the corridor of heaviest snow, highlighted by 1.98 at Holdrege. As is typical of April snow, it did not stick around long as high temperatures two days later bounced back into the 60s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 was eased across the northwest as convection brought rainfall. Further south and east, the drought intensified into portions of the sandhills and north central to Extreme (D3) conditions and Severe (D2) conditions northeast into eastern Holt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 eased in May. The Severe (D2) drought conditions across the south were reduced to Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions to Moderate (D1) conditions. The county received an average of 2 to 8 inches of rainfall during the month, with the highest amounts across the northwest.

Further south and east, the drought intensified into portions of the sandhills and north central to Extreme (D3) conditions and Severe (D2) conditions northeast into eastern Holt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began in June 2012 intensified slightly across the north in May. Severe (D2) drought conditions were seen across the entire county. The county received an average of 1 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. The drought continued into June.
